      "new_str": "      <!-- ============================== REFERENCES ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\" data-section=\"references\">\n        <h1 id=\"references\">References</h1>\n        <p class=\"docs-lede\">\n          Sources for the algorithms, physical models, and numerical\n          methods used in Washes. The Dig Deeper section below\n          walks through how each one shaped the lib's evolution.\n        </p>\n        <ol class=\"docs-references\">\n          <li>\n            <strong>Curtis, C. J., Anderson, S. E., Seims, J. E., Fleischer, K. W., &amp; Salesin, D. H.</strong>\n            (1997). Computer-generated watercolor. In <em>Proceedings of the 24th annual conference on Computer graphics and interactive techniques</em> (SIGGRAPH '97), pp. 421\u2013430. ACM Press/Addison-Wesley.\n            <span class=\"docs-cite-note\">doi: 10.1145/258734.258896. Paper PDF available at <a href=\"https://grail.cs.washington.edu/projects/watercolor/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">grail.cs.washington.edu/projects/watercolor</a>.</span>\n            <em>The foundation paper Washes is built on. Three-layer water/pigment/paper model, capillary diffusion, edge darkening, and Kubelka-Munk compositing. Figure 5 lists the K and S coefficients per pigment that ship in this lib.</em>\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>Kubelka, P. &amp; Munk, F.</strong>\n            (1931). Ein Beitrag zur Optik der Farbanstriche. <em>Zeitschrift f\u00fcr Technische Physik</em>, 12, 593\u2013601.\n            <span class=\"docs-cite-note\">English translation by S. Westin available at <a href=\"https://www.graphics.cornell.edu/~westin/pubs/kubelka.pdf\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">graphics.cornell.edu/~westin/pubs/kubelka.pdf</a>. Original German title roughly: \"A contribution to the optics of paint layers\".</span>\n            <em>The two-flux radiative transfer model that gives watercolor its luminous, glaze-able look. Each pigment is characterized by an absorption coefficient K and a scattering coefficient S; the reflectance of a layer of thickness x over a background of reflectance Rbg is a closed-form function of K, S, x, and Rbg.</em>\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>Foster, N. &amp; Metaxas, D.</strong>\n            (1996). Realistic animation of liquids. <em>Graphical Models and Image Processing</em>, 58(5), 471\u2013483.\n            <span class=\"docs-cite-note\">doi: 10.1006/gmip.1996.0039. Originally presented at Graphics Interface '96.</span>\n            <em>The first Navier-Stokes-based fluid solver targeted at computer graphics \u2014 staggered grid (MAC), explicit time integration, finite-difference Navier-Stokes. Curtis et al. cite this as the basis for their shallow-water step, which is the basis for the velocity field in Washes.</em>\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>Stam, J.</strong>\n            (1999). Stable Fluids. In <em>Proceedings of the 26th Annual Conference on Computer Graphics and Interactive Techniques</em> (SIGGRAPH '99), pp. 121\u2013128. ACM Press/Addison-Wesley.\n            <span class=\"docs-cite-note\">Paper available at <a href=\"https://pages.cs.wisc.edu/~chaol/data/cs777/stam-stable_fluids.pdf\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">pages.cs.wisc.edu/~chaol/data/cs777/stam-stable_fluids.pdf</a>.</span>\n            <em>Introduced semi-Lagrangian advection to the graphics community. The \"stable\" in the title refers to the scheme being unconditionally stable at any timestep \u2014 no CFL bound to satisfy. This is the advection scheme that finally fixed the cross artifact in Washes v0.67, after 11 versions chasing the wrong root cause.</em>\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>Bridson, R.</strong>\n            (2015). <em>Fluid Simulation for Computer Graphics</em> (2nd ed.). AK Peters/CRC Press. ISBN: 978-1482232837.\n            <span class=\"docs-cite-note\">Publisher page: <a href=\"https://www.routledge.com/Fluid-Simulation-for-Computer-Graphics/Bridson/p/book/9781482232837\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">routledge.com</a></span>\n            <em>Textbook reference for everything in this lib's fluid pipeline. The Advection Algorithms chapter (semi-Lagrangian, donor-cell, MacCormack) and the chapter on Making Fluids Incompressible are particularly relevant to the v0.67-0.72 work.</em>\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>Courant, R., Friedrichs, K., &amp; Lewy, H.</strong>\n            (1928). \u00dcber die partiellen Differenzengleichungen der mathematischen Physik. <em>Mathematische Annalen</em>, 100(1), 32\u201374.\n            <span class=\"docs-cite-note\">doi: 10.1007/BF01448839. English translation: \"On the Partial Difference Equations of Mathematical Physics\", <em>IBM Journal of Research and Development</em>, 11(2), 215\u2013234 (1967). The English translation is available at <a href=\"https://web.stanford.edu/class/cme324/classics/courant-friedrichs-lewy.pdf\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">web.stanford.edu/class/cme324/classics/courant-friedrichs-lewy.pdf</a>.</span>\n            <em>The CFL condition: an explicit time-marching scheme is unstable when the numerical domain of dependence is smaller than the physical one. For donor-cell advection on a grid with spacing 1, the condition is <code>|u|\u00b7\u0394t + |v|\u00b7\u0394t \u2264 1</code> per cell. Violating it gives the cross artifact's negative-pigment streaks in Washes pre-v0.69.</em>\n          </li>\n        </ol>\n      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== DIG DEEPER ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\" data-section=\"dig-deeper\">\n        <h1 id=\"dig-deeper\">Dig deeper</h1>\n        <p class=\"docs-lede\">\n          The math and physics that shaped Washes, with pointers to\n          the source material. Citations are numbered to the References\n          section above. Most of this content emerged from chasing one\n          specific artifact &mdash; the cross-shape that appeared in\n          deluge splashes &mdash; across thirteen versions before\n          finding its actual root cause.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"dig-overview\">What's actually being simulated</h2>\n        <p>\n          Following Curtis et al. <a href=\"#references\" class=\"docs-cite-ref\">[1]</a>, Washes maintains three layers per\n          pigment channel on a 2D grid (in this lib, three channels):\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>The shallow-water layer</strong> &mdash; velocity field <em>(u, v)</em>, pressure <em>p</em>, and \"wet\" mask. Pigment dissolved in this layer moves with the water flow.</li>\n          <li><strong>The pigment-deposition layer</strong> &mdash; tracks <em>g<sub>k</sub></em> (granular/suspended pigment, advects) vs <em>d<sub>k</sub></em> (deposited pigment, stays put) for each pigment k.</li>\n          <li><strong>The paper layer</strong> &mdash; capillary diffusion field; controls how moisture spreads through paper fibers.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <p>\n          The render pipeline composites these via the Kubelka-Munk\n          reflectance model <a href=\"#references\" class=\"docs-cite-ref\">[2]</a>: each cell becomes a layer of given\n          thickness over a paper-colored background, and the layer's\n          reflectance R is computed from its K and S coefficients.\n          Pigments listed in Figure 5 of Curtis et al. <a href=\"#references\" class=\"docs-cite-ref\">[1]</a> (used here\n          unmodified) have direction-dependent K and S that produce\n          glaze-like layered transparency.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"dig-advection\">The advection equation and why it had a cross</h2>\n        <p>\n          For pigment density <em>g</em> moving in a velocity field\n          <em>(u, v)</em>, conservation of pigment mass gives the\n          advection equation:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-math\">\u2202g/\u2202t + u \u00b7 \u2202g/\u2202x + v \u00b7 \u2202g/\u2202y = 0</pre>\n        <p>\n          The first-order donor-cell (upwind) discretization that\n          Washes started with computes the per-cell flux as\n          <code>flux = |u|\u00b7g\u00b7\u0394t</code> for the upwind axis, with\n          similar for v. The scheme is mass-conserving and easy to\n          implement, but it has two well-known issues:\n        </p>\n        <ol class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>CFL bound.</strong> The scheme is conditionally stable: <code>|u|\u00b7\u0394t + |v|\u00b7\u0394t \u2264 1</code> per cell. Above that bound, the cell donates more pigment than it holds, goes negative, and Kubelka-Munk renders negative values as brighter-than-paper. The condition is Courant, Friedrichs, &amp; Lewy 1928 <a href=\"#references\" class=\"docs-cite-ref\">[6]</a>.</li>\n          <li><strong>Cardinal anisotropy.</strong> Even at CFL \u2264 1, cells with pure-cardinal velocity (V, 0) donate to one neighbor while diagonal cells (V/\u221a2, V/\u221a2) split across two. The L1 norm (|u| + |v|) used in the CFL bound differs between these by \u221a2, and the discretization itself has subtle direction-dependent transport rates. See Bridson <a href=\"#references\" class=\"docs-cite-ref\">[5]</a>, Advection Algorithms chapter.</li>\n        </ol>\n        <p>\n          Stam's semi-Lagrangian advection <a href=\"#references\" class=\"docs-cite-ref\">[4]</a> sidesteps both problems by\n          changing the integration direction. Instead of moving\n          pigment forward from each cell along its velocity vector\n          (donor-cell), it traces <em>backward</em> from each cell\n          along the velocity vector to find where the pigment \"came\n          from\" one step ago, then samples that source position via\n          bilinear interpolation:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-math\">For each cell (x, y):\n  src_x = x - u(x,y) \u00b7 \u0394t\n  src_y = y - v(x,y) \u00b7 \u0394t\n  g_new(x, y) = bilinear_sample(g, src_x, src_y)</pre>\n        <p>\n          The bilinear interpolation function has no preferred axis\n          &mdash; it weights neighbors by continuous proximity rather\n          than per-axis flux. So the scheme is isotropic by\n          construction. And because <em>src_x</em> and <em>src_y</em>\n          can be anywhere in the grid, there's no CFL bound: the\n          scheme is unconditionally stable. The trade-off is slight\n          numerical diffusion from the bilinear smoothing, which for\n          watercolor reads as natural soft blending.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"dig-mass-conservation\">Mass conservation vs density conservation</h2>\n        <p>\n          Plain semi-Lagrangian preserves <em>density</em> (value\n          per cell), but for a divergent flow this isn't what you\n          want. Consider a small fluid parcel in a radial outflow:\n          its area expands over time, so the pigment originally\n          covering 1 cell now covers (say) 1.1 cells. The total mass\n          is constant, but the density has dropped to 1/1.1.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Plain semi-Lagrangian doesn't see this: each cell samples\n          a single backward source and keeps that source's density.\n          For a deluge, that produced the wrong visual &mdash; the\n          splash appeared to redistribute pigment uniformly instead\n          of clearing the center and accumulating at the wave front.\n          Washes v0.68 fixed it with a divergence correction:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-math\">div(v) = \u2202u/\u2202x + \u2202v/\u2202y    \u2190 computed via central differences\narea_ratio = exp(-div \u00b7 \u0394t)   \u2190 Jacobian of the backward map\ng_new = bilinear_sample \u00b7 area_ratio</pre>\n        <p>\n          In a divergent region (\u2207\u00b7v &gt; 0, fluid spreading out),\n          <code>area_ratio &lt; 1</code> so density drops; in a\n          convergent region (\u2207\u00b7v &lt; 0, fluid piling up),\n          <code>area_ratio &gt; 1</code> so density grows. This\n          restores the \"lift and reveal\" feel of a real watercolor\n          deluge. The <code>exp()</code> form rather than\n          <code>(1 - div\u00b7\u0394t)</code> matters for stability when\n          divergence is large (e.g. near the splash center, \u2207\u00b7v ~ 2V/r\n          becomes large for small r). See Bridson <a href=\"#references\" class=\"docs-cite-ref\">[5]</a> for the\n          mass-conservative formulation.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"dig-velocity-clamp\">The square vs circular velocity clamp (a \u221a2 bug hiding in plain sight)</h2>\n        <p>\n          For 13 versions, I was chasing the cross artifact in the\n          pigment advection code. The artifact persisted in every\n          mode (donor-cell, flux-clamped, substep-CFL-safe, semi-\n          Lagrangian, mass-conserving semi-Lagrangian). The clue I\n          finally noticed in v0.69 was that the velocity field itself\n          was anisotropic one step after the splash:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-math\">After 1 simStep at default deluge (v0.68):\n  Cardinal cells (E,W,N,S): velocity magnitude = 1.14\n  Diagonal cells (NE,NW,SE,SW): velocity magnitude = 1.62\n\n  Ratio: 1.62 / 1.14 = 1.42  \u2248  \u221a2</pre>\n        <p>\n          The cause was the velocity clamp in updateVelocity:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">if (nu &gt;  VEL_CLAMP) nu =  VEL_CLAMP;\nif (nu &lt; -VEL_CLAMP) nu = -VEL_CLAMP;\nif (nv &gt;  VEL_CLAMP) nv =  VEL_CLAMP;\nif (nv &lt; -VEL_CLAMP) nv = -VEL_CLAMP;</pre>\n        <p>\n          Each axis was clamped independently to \u00b1VEL_CLAMP. The\n          allowed region in (u, v) space is a SQUARE\n          [-CLAMP, CLAMP]\u00b2 rather than a circle. The four corners of\n          that square (the diagonal directions) reach magnitude\n          \u221a2\u00b7CLAMP, while the four edge midpoints (the cardinal\n          directions) reach magnitude CLAMP. Diagonal directions got\n          \u221a2\u00d7 the speed of cardinal directions at all times. The\n          pigment was advecting through an already-anisotropic\n          velocity field; every pigment-advection scheme I tried\n          inherited that anisotropy and produced a cross.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          The fix is magnitude clamping: bound the L2 norm rather\n          than each axis independently.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">const mag = Math.sqrt(nu*nu + nv*nv);\nif (mag &gt; VEL_CLAMP) {\n  const s = VEL_CLAMP / mag;\n  nu *= s;  nv *= s;\n}</pre>\n        <p>\n          Direction is preserved (uniform scale factor on both\n          components); magnitude is bounded isotropically (the\n          allowed region is now a circle of radius CLAMP); all eight\n          compass directions get the same maximum speed.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"dig-singular-cells\">Singular cells at the splash epicenter</h2>\n        <p>\n          The last residual artifact (v0.70-0.72) was a dark pinpoint\n          at the click site. Tracing it isolated the cause to the\n          radial-velocity injection guard:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">if (d2 &gt; r2 || d2 &lt; 0.5) continue;\nconst dist = Math.sqrt(d2);\nconst inv = 1 / dist;\nconst radialX = dx * inv;\nconst radialY = dy * inv;</pre>\n        <p>\n          The <code>d\u00b2 &lt; 0.5</code> threshold was meant to avoid\n          <code>1/0</code> at the exact epicenter (where\n          <em>dx=dy=0</em>). But <code>1/0</code> only happens at\n          exactly <em>d\u00b2 = 0</em>, possible only when the epicenter\n          falls at integer-valued coordinates and a grid cell sits\n          exactly there. Cells with small but nonzero d\u00b2 (e.g. 0.25)\n          have perfectly well-defined unit-radial vectors. The 0.5\n          threshold was throwing away 1\u20132 cells' worth of valid\n          injection around every splash, leaving them in a singular\n          state (zero velocity, zero pressure, symmetric neighborhood\n          so the Navier-Stokes update couldn't perturb them off zero).\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Several frames into the simulation, this caused something\n          unexpected. The remaining pressure injection decays as\n          <code>p \u2190 0.94\u00b7p</code> per step, leaving a pressure dip\n          (zero where neighbors are at the injection value). Pressure\n          gradients drive flow from high to low pressure, so once the\n          initial outward velocity damped, the dip became the\n          dominant gradient and started <em>pulling</em> neighbors'\n          velocity INTO the singular cell:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-math\">After 10 simSteps (v0.71, before threshold fix):\n  Singular cell (324,271):  u=+0.57, v=+0.10\n  West neighbor (323,271):  u=+1.12  \u2190 reversed, pointing INTO singular\n  East neighbor (325,271):  u=-1.12  \u2190 reversed, pointing INTO singular\n  North neighbor (324,270): v=+1.14  \u2190 reversed, pointing INTO singular\n\n  Pigment accumulated at singular cell: 5\u00d7 the surrounding density.</pre>\n        <p>\n          The fix is at the source. Tighten the guard to\n          <code>d\u00b2 &lt; 1e-10</code>: a safe margin against genuine\n          <code>1/0</code> (far below floating-point precision risk)\n          but no longer over-aggressive. All cells with valid radial\n          directions now receive proper injection, so there's no\n          singularity to begin with, and no pressure dip can form.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"dig-debug-lessons\">Debugging fluid simulations &mdash; lessons</h2>\n        <p>\n          A few principles I should have applied earlier in this\n          project, in case you (or any future maintainer, including\n          another AI) hit similar issues:\n        </p>\n        <ol class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>When an artifact has a strong axis-aligned shape, the artifact's appearance is real evidence &mdash; but it doesn't identify which subsystem produced it.</strong> The cross looked like a donor-cell advection signature, so I lived in the pigment-advection code for 13 versions. The actual source was the velocity clamp, one function upstream. Symptoms can be inherited downstream from an unrelated subsystem.</li>\n          <li><strong>Dump numerical state, don't just stare at renders.</strong> The cross diagnosis took 13 versions visually but would have taken 5 minutes if I'd printed <code>{cardinal_velocity, diagonal_velocity}</code> after one step. Rendered output is the convolution of every layer of the sim; numerical dumps isolate the layers.</li>\n          <li><strong>Fix the source, don't patch the symptom.</strong> The pinpoint had three rounds of patches (v0.70 single-cell smooth, v0.71 multi-cell smooth, v0.72 fix the threshold). Each patch was closer to \"don't break it in the first place\" and further from \"patch the damage afterward.\" Lesson: when a fix targets the consequences of a guard or threshold, check whether the guard or threshold itself is what's wrong.</li>\n          <li><strong>Test the boundary cases, not just the convenient ones.</strong> v0.70 worked for the integer-coordinate \"Deluge Now\" button but failed for random clicks with offset-fractional coordinates. The bug was in a specific subset of input that I hadn't included in my test cases.</li>\n          <li><strong>Conserved quantities are diagnostic.</strong> Mass conservation, momentum conservation, and isotropy can be checked numerically and used as invariants. Any drift from those invariants over many frames is a signal pointing at a specific subsystem.</li>\n        </ol>\n\n        <h2 id=\"dig-further-reading\">Further reading</h2>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>For more on the K-M model and its limitations on dyed/highly-absorbing papers, see <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kubelka%E2%80%93Munk_theory\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">the Wikipedia K-M article</a> (a good overview) and Westin's translation of the original 1931 paper <a href=\"https://www.graphics.cornell.edu/~westin/pubs/kubelka.pdf\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">[2]</a>.</li>\n          <li>For more on semi-Lagrangian and the broader fluid-solver landscape, the canonical modern textbook is Bridson <a href=\"#references\" class=\"docs-cite-ref\">[5]</a>. Stam's \"Stable Fluids\" paper <a href=\"#references\" class=\"docs-cite-ref\">[4]</a> is short, readable, and remains the simplest introduction to the technique. Dan Morris has an even-more-introductory annotated walkthrough at <a href=\"https://dmorris.net/projects/summaries/dmorris.stable_fluids.notes.pdf\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">dmorris.net</a>.</li>\n          <li>For more on Curtis et al.'s shallow-water approach in particular, the SIGGRAPH '97 paper <a href=\"#references\" class=\"docs-cite-ref\">[1]</a> is the source. Yann Semet's <a href=\"https://www.slideshare.net/ysemet/computer-generated-watercolor-1858806\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">slide presentation</a> gives a good high-level overview.</li>\n          <li>For more on the CFL condition and its modern descendents (von Neumann analysis, Lax equivalence), <em>The Courant-Friedrichs-Lewy (CFL) Condition: 80 Years After Its Discovery</em> (Kubrusly &amp; de Moura, eds., Birkh\u00e4user 2012) collects current applications and historical perspective.</li>\n        </ul>\n      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== CHANGELOG ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\" data-section=\"changelog\">\n        <h1 id=\"changelog\">Changelog</h1>\n        <p class=\"docs-lede\">\n          Reverse-chronological release history. Each version is a\n          standalone <code>.html</code> file kept in <code>/mnt/user-data/outputs/</code>\n          so you can always go back to a previous version's exact behavior.\n        </p>\n\n",
      "old_str": "      <!-- ============================== REFERENCES ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\" data-section=\"references\">\n        <h1 id=\"references\">References</h1>\n        <ol class=\"docs-references\">\n          <li>\n            <strong>Curtis, C. J., Anderson, S. E., Seims, J. E., Fleischer, K. W., &amp; Salesin, D. H.</strong>\n            \"Computer-Generated Watercolor.\" SIGGRAPH '97 Proceedings.\n            <em>The foundation paper. Three-layer water/pigment/paper model with capillary diffusion, edge darkening, and Kubelka-Munk compositing. Figure 5 lists the K and S values used here.</em>\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>Foster, N., &amp; Metaxas, D.</strong>\n            \"Realistic Animation of Liquids.\" Graphics Interface '96.\n            <em>Staggered-grid fluid scheme; basis for the velocity update.</em>\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>Stam, J.</strong>\n            \"Stable Fluids.\" SIGGRAPH '99.\n            <em>Modern numerical-stability conventions for the advection step.</em>\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>Kubelka, P.</strong>\n            \"New contributions to the optics of intensely light-scattering materials.\" JOSA 1954.\n            <em>The reflectance compositing formula used in the render path.</em>\n          </li>\n        </ol>\n      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== CHANGELOG ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\" data-section=\"changelog\">\n        <h1 id=\"changelog\">Changelog</h1>\n        <p class=\"docs-lede\">\n          Reverse-chronological release history. Each version is a\n          standalone <code>.html</code> file kept in <code>/mnt/user-data/outputs/</code>\n          so you can always go back to a previous version's exact behavior.\n        </p>\n\n",

  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-13T02:07:45.677052Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-13T02:07:58.435550Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "tool_use",
    "id": "toolu_01S6iCc1fxsosGG7QXVF15GH",
    "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "Add v0.73 changelog entry for the docs work",
      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.73 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-73\">v0.73 &mdash; Documentation pass: References + Dig Deeper</h2>\n        <p>\n          No simulation changes &mdash; just documentation. After\n          the v0.72 root-cause pinpoint fix the sim is in a stable\n          place, so this version captures what we learned during the\n          v0.56-0.72 arc.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Expanded References section</h3>\n        <p>\n          Full bibliographic entries with DOIs and URLs for the six\n          primary sources behind Washes:\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>Curtis et al. (1997)</strong> \u2014 the SIGGRAPH '97 watercolor paper this lib is built on. doi: 10.1145/258734.258896.</li>\n          <li><strong>Kubelka &amp; Munk (1931)</strong> \u2014 the optical compositing model. Published in Zeitschrift f\u00fcr Technische Physik; English translation by S. Westin at graphics.cornell.edu.</li>\n          <li><strong>Foster &amp; Metaxas (1996)</strong> \u2014 first NS-based fluid solver in graphics; basis for the velocity update. doi: 10.1006/gmip.1996.0039.</li>\n          <li><strong>Stam (1999)</strong> \u2014 Stable Fluids, the semi-Lagrangian advection scheme that fixed the cross.</li>\n          <li><strong>Bridson (2015)</strong> \u2014 the standard textbook for fluid simulation in graphics. ISBN 978-1482232837.</li>\n          <li><strong>Courant, Friedrichs, Lewy (1928)</strong> \u2014 the CFL condition. doi: 10.1007/BF01448839.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <h3>New \"Dig deeper\" section</h3>\n        <p>\n          A walk-through of the math and physics that shaped the v0.56-v0.72 arc:\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>What's being simulated</strong> \u2014 Curtis et al.'s three-layer model (shallow water, pigment deposition, paper diffusion) and K-M compositing.</li>\n          <li><strong>The advection equation</strong> \u2014 the \u2202g/\u2202t + v\u00b7\u2207g = 0 PDE, donor-cell discretization, CFL bound, the cardinal-axis anisotropy, and how Stam's semi-Lagrangian sidesteps both.</li>\n          <li><strong>Mass conservation</strong> \u2014 why plain semi-Lagrangian doesn't conserve mass in divergent flow, and the Jacobian-of-the-backward-map fix (the exp(-div\u00b7\u0394t) area ratio in v0.68).</li>\n          <li><strong>Square vs circular clamp</strong> \u2014 the \u221a2 anisotropy that the per-axis velocity clamp was injecting, with numerical evidence. The actual root cause of the cross artifact after 13 versions.</li>\n          <li><strong>Singular cells</strong> \u2014 why the over-aggressive injection guard left cells in a degenerate state, the pressure-dip-induced velocity reversal that turned them into pigment sinks, and the one-character threshold fix.</li>\n          <li><strong>Debugging lessons</strong> \u2014 five principles I should have applied earlier in this project, distilled from the artifacts I chased.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <p>\n          All citations cross-referenced inline.\n        </p>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.72 ===== -->",
      "old_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.72 ===== -->",
      "path": "/home/claude/watercolor.html"
    },
    "message": "Add v0.73 changelog entry for the docs work",

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "Shipped v0.73 with the documentation. Here's what's in there:\n\n## Updated References section\n\nSix primary sources, all verified via web search, with full bibliographic details:\n\n1. **Curtis et al. (1997)** \u2014 SIGGRAPH '97. doi: 10.1145/258734.258896. Paper PDF at grail.cs.washington.edu.\n2. **Kubelka & Munk (1931)** \u2014 *Zeitschrift f\u00fcr Technische Physik*, 12, 593\u2013601. English translation by S. Westin linked.\n3. **Foster & Metaxas (1996)** \u2014 *Graphical Models and Image Processing*, 58(5), 471\u2013483. doi: 10.1006/gmip.1996.0039.\n4. **Stam (1999)** \u2014 Stable Fluids, SIGGRAPH '99.\n5. **Bridson (2015)** \u2014 *Fluid Simulation for Computer Graphics*, 2nd ed. ISBN 978-1482232837.\n6. **Courant, Friedrichs & Lewy (1928)** \u2014 *Mathematische Annalen*, 100(1), 32\u201374. doi: 10.1007/BF01448839. English translation at web.stanford.edu.\n\nEach entry has a one-paragraph note explaining how it shaped Washes specifically \u2014 not generic descriptions.\n\n## New \"Dig deeper\" section\n\nSix subsections, each cross-referencing the citations inline:\n\n- **What's being simulated** \u2014 Curtis et al.'s three-layer model and K-M compositing\n- **The advection equation and why it had a cross** \u2014 the \u2202g/\u2202t + v\u00b7\u2207g = 0 PDE, donor-cell vs semi-Lagrangian, the CFL bound, cardinal anisotropy\n- **Mass conservation vs density conservation** \u2014 Jacobian-of-the-backward-map correction (exp(-div\u00b7\u0394t)) and why it matters for the deluge's \"lift and reveal\"\n- **The square vs circular velocity clamp** \u2014 the \u221a2 anisotropy bug with numerical evidence (1.62 / 1.14 \u2248 \u221a2), the actual root cause of the cross after 13 versions\n- **Singular cells at the splash epicenter** \u2014 the pressure-dip-induced velocity reversal, with the numerical trace showing neighbors' velocities flipping direction after 10 frames\n- **Debugging fluid simulations \u2014 lessons** \u2014 five principles distilled from chasing the cross for 13 versions: artifacts can be inherited downstream, dump numerical state, fix the source not the symptom, test boundary cases, use conservation laws as diagnostics\n\nThe nav under \"References\" now has sub-entries for each subsection so it's easy to jump around.\n\n## v0.73 changelog entry\n\nDocumentation-only release. The simulation code is untouched from v0.72.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

The plan is to add this section to the docs and save a standalone test file to outputs so users have both the reference material and working code to build from.\n\nFor the testing section, I'm describing how to run the library headless in Node by mocking out the DOM layer, since the core simulation is pure JavaScript arrays and math...\n\nInjecting debug helpers via string replacement before evaluating the library code to expose internal state without modifying the public API...\n\nWriting code examples that show three debugging patterns: tracing a single cell across frames to catch velocity reversals, comparing cardinal versus diagonal behavior to detect anisotropy artifacts, and scanning all cells for anomalous values to catch edge cases that survive multiple fix attempts...\n\nThen wrapping up with best practices for working on fluid simulations \u2014 the core principle being that numerical inspection is essential alongside visual debugging.\n\nNow I'm thinking through the practical guidelines: watching for the \u221a2 ratio as a red flag for norm confusion, testing fractional coordinate edge cases, fixing root causes rather than symptoms, using conservation laws to pinpoint broken subsystems, and recognizing that visual artifacts can originate several steps upstream from where they appear.\n\nI should actually move forward with this now and check what tools are available, though I'm still deciding whether to add this as a new section or integrate it elsewhere.\n\nBuilding out the test infrastructure...\n\nI'm consolidating the diagnostic patterns into a single unified test file that covers the hotspot scanning, cell tracing, and anisotropy detection\u2014this gives the user a complete, runnable example that demonstrates all the key techniques we developed.\n\nNow I need to think about how to document the journey itself. The user provided screenshots showing where bugs surfaced across different versions, so I should create a section that ties those visual reports to the actual fixes that came from them. I'll structure this as a maintainer's guide that walks through the timeline of issues and what each one taught us about the algorithm's behavior. The \"every three clicks\" pattern held even after tracing cell state through 30 frames \u2014 velocities were reversing direction, which was the smoking gun. Now I'm setting up a test harness that loads the library and exposes diagnostic functions, along with patterns for instrumenting the code, tracing cell behavior, detecting anisotropy, and scanning for hotspots. I'll make the harness self-contained and runnable, then ship both the section content and the test file together.\n\nActually, I'm reconsidering the audience here \u2014 if someone's integrating the library, they don't need test code at all, but if they're working on the library itself, they want reproducible cases and templates for their own tests. I'll focus the documentation on the wisdom we've learned and offer the test harness as an optional separate file. The screenshots are physical proof of what each bug looked like, though embedding them directly is tricky.\n\nMy plan is to expand the docs HTML with a new \"Testing patterns\" subsection under \"Dig Deeper\" (which already has debugging lessons), include the timeline of visual reports, lay out the best practices, and save the test harness separately. I need to figure out whether to integrate this into the existing structure or create a new top-level \"For maintainers\" section \u2014 I think keeping it within \"Dig Deeper\" makes sense since that's already the deep-dive area.\n\nLet me finalize the structure: two new subsections under \"Dig Deeper\" for the timeline with screenshot references and the testing patterns, expand the existing debugging lessons, save test-harness.js as a standalone file, and bump to v0.74.     "start_timestamp": "2026-05-13T02:45:58.517715Z",

    "type": "tool_use",
    "id": "toolu_01Wywu4WtCqCjfXjhLe7xrdi",
    "name": "create_file",
    "input": {
      "description": "Standalone Node.js test harness with the diagnostic patterns from the v0.56-0.72 arc",
      "file_text": "// washes-test-harness.js\n//\n// Headless Node.js test harness for the Washes watercolor lib,\n// extracted from the diagnostic scripts that drove the v0.56-v0.72\n// bug-hunting arc.\n//\n// The lib targets browsers but its core simulation is pure JS arrays\n// and math. This harness stubs the DOM/Canvas APIs so the lib can be\n// loaded in Node, then injects per-test `_debug_*` helpers via string\n// replacement before eval'ing the source. The result: a few seconds\n// of numerical inspection instead of staring at rendered output for\n// 13 versions.\n//\n// USAGE:\n//   node washes-test-harness.js  /path/to/watercolor-lib.js  [pattern]\n//\n// PATTERNS:\n//   anisotropy     \u2014 cardinal vs diagonal advection ratio (v0.69 \u221a2 bug)\n//   trace          \u2014 single-cell state over many frames  (v0.72 pinpoint)\n//   hotspots       \u2014 scan grid for cells differing significantly from\n//                    their neighbors (v0.71-0.72 verification)\n//   cfl            \u2014 CFL stability bound for explicit advection\n//   all            \u2014 run all patterns\n//\n// EXAMPLE:\n//   node washes-test-harness.js ./watercolor-lib.js anisotropy\n//\n// This file is meant to be copied and adapted \u2014 each pattern below\n// is a self-contained example of the diagnostic technique it was\n// named after.\n\n'use strict';\n\nconst fs = require('fs');\n\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n// Mock DOM. Just enough to get window.Washes.create() to run. None of these\n// stubs do anything useful visually \u2014 they just satisfy the lib's bootstrap\n// code that touches document/window/canvas APIs.\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\nfunction makeEl(tag) {\n  const e = {\n    tagName: (tag || 'div').toUpperCase(),\n    nodeName: (tag || 'div').toUpperCase(),\n    attributes: {},\n    children: [],\n    childNodes: [],\n    style: {},\n    dataset: {},\n    parentNode: null,\n    parentElement: null,\n    width: 1024,\n    height: 768,\n    _listeners: {},\n    ownerSVGElement: null,\n    classList: {\n      toggle() {}, add() {}, remove() {}, contains() { return false; },\n    },\n    setAttribute(n, v) { this.attributes[n] = String(v); },\n    getAttribute(n) {\n      return Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(this.attributes, n)\n        ? this.attributes[n] : null;\n    },\n    appendChild(c) {\n      this.children.push(c);\n      this.childNodes.push(c);\n      c.parentNode = this;\n      c.parentElement = this;\n      return c;\n    },\n    removeChild(c) {\n      this.children = this.children.filter((x) => x !== c);\n      return c;\n    },\n    replaceChildren() {\n      this.children = [];\n      this.childNodes = [];\n    },\n    addEventListener(t, fn) {\n      this._listeners[t] = this._listeners[t] || [];\n      this._listeners[t].push(fn);\n    },\n    removeEventListener() {},\n    dispatchEvent(ev) {\n      (this._listeners[ev.type] || []).forEach((f) => f(ev));\n      return true;\n    },\n    setPointerCapture() {},\n    releasePointerCapture() {},\n    getBoundingClientRect() {\n      return { left: 0, top: 0, right: 1080, bottom: 900, width: 1080, height: 900, x: 0, y: 0 };\n    },\n    toDataURL() { return 'data:image/png;base64,F'; },\n    toBlob(cb) { setTimeout(() => cb({ size: 1, type: 'image/png' }), 0); },\n    querySelector() { return null; },\n    querySelectorAll() { return []; },\n    getContext(t) {\n      if (t === 'webgl2') return null;\n      return {\n        createImageData: (w, h) => ({ data: new Uint8ClampedArray(w * h * 4), width: w, height: h }),\n        getImageData: (x, y, w, h) => ({ data: new Uint8ClampedArray(w * h * 4), width: w, height: h }),\n        putImageData() {}, drawImage() {}, clearRect() {}, fillRect() {}, fillText() {},\n        measureText() { return { width: 50 }; },\n        save() {}, restore() {}, translate() {}, rotate() {}, scale() {},\n        imageSmoothingEnabled: true, imageSmoothingQuality: 'high',\n        fillStyle: '', strokeStyle: '', font: '',\n        textBaseline: '', textAlign: '',\n        globalAlpha: 1, globalCompositeOperation: 'source-over',\n      };\n    },\n  };\n  return e;\n}\n\nfunction installMockDOM() {\n  global.document = {\n    _body: makeEl('body'),\n    createElement: (t) => makeEl(t),\n    createElementNS: (ns, t) => makeEl(t),\n    getElementById: () => null,\n    querySelectorAll: () => [],\n    documentElement: { style: { setProperty() {} }, dataset: {} },\n  };\n  global.document.body = global.document._body;\n  global.window = {\n    innerWidth: 1080,\n    innerHeight: 900,\n    devicePixelRatio: 1,\n    addEventListener() {},\n    location: { search: '' },\n    requestAnimationFrame: () => 0,\n    cancelAnimationFrame: () => {},\n    matchMedia: () => ({ matches: false }),\n  };\n  global.navigator = { maxTouchPoints: 0 };\n  global.requestAnimationFrame = () => 0;\n  global.cancelAnimationFrame = () => {};\n  global.performance = { now: () => Date.now() };\n  global.URLSearchParams = URLSearchParams;\n  global.URL = { createObjectURL: () => 'blob:fake', revokeObjectURL: () => {} };\n  global.Blob = function () {};\n  global.DOMParser = function () {\n    return { parseFromString: () => ({ querySelector: () => null, querySelectorAll: () => [] }) };\n  };\n  global.Image = function () {};\n  global.CustomEvent = function (t, o) { this.type = t; this.detail = o && o.detail; };\n}\n\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n// Load the lib with debug helpers injected. The trick: inject `_debug_*`\n// methods into the public API object via string replacement on the source,\n// then eval. This avoids modifying the lib file on disk and gives each test\n// scoped access to internal state (g, d, u, v, wet, pressure, etc.).\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\nfunction loadLibWithHelpers(libPath, extraMethods) {\n  const src = fs.readFileSync(libPath, 'utf8');\n  const patched = src.replace('  state() {', extraMethods + '\\n  state() {');\n  new Function(patched)();\n  return global.window.Washes;\n}\n\n// ===========================================================================\n// PATTERN 1 \u2014 Anisotropy detection\n// ===========================================================================\n// This is the test that found the v0.69 cross-artifact root cause. After 13\n// versions of patching the pigment advection code (donor-cell, flux clamp,\n// substep CFL, semi-Lagrangian, mass-conserving semi-Lagrangian), the cross\n// still appeared. Comparing pigment density at distance d in cardinal vs\n// diagonal directions revealed a ratio of \u221a2 \u2014 the tell of an L1-vs-L2 norm\n// confusion. Root cause: per-axis velocity clamp instead of magnitude clamp.\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\nfunction runAnisotropy(libPath) {\n  installMockDOM();\n  const Washes = loadLibWithHelpers(libPath, `\n    _debug_run(n) {\n      for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) { movePigment(); transferPigment(); }\n    },\n    _debug_anisotropy(d) {\n      const cx = Math.floor(GW / 2), cy = Math.floor(GH / 2);\n      const dd = Math.round(d / Math.SQRT2);\n      function get(x, y) {\n        return g[0][y * GW + x] + g[1][y * GW + x] + g[2][y * GW + x];\n      }\n      const card = (get(cx + d, cy) + get(cx - d, cy) + get(cx, cy - d) + get(cx, cy + d)) / 4;\n      const diag = (get(cx + dd, cy - dd) + get(cx - dd, cy - dd) + get(cx + dd, cy + dd) + get(cx - dd, cy + dd)) / 4;\n      return { card, diag, ratio: diag > 0 ? card / diag : Infinity };\n    },\n    _debug_paintGrid(amt) {\n      for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) g[0][i] = amt;\n      setActiveRectFull();\n    },`);\n\n  const scenarios = [\n    { vel: 40, label: 'velocity=40 (default deluge)' },\n    { vel: 10, label: 'velocity=10 (CFL safe)' },\n    { vel: 5,  label: 'velocity=5  (well below CFL)' },\n  ];\n\n  console.log('\\n=== Anisotropy (cardinal/diagonal ratio at d=100) ===');\n  console.log('  ratio = 1.0 is isotropic; ~1.4 = \u221a2 = per-axis L1 norm bug');\n  for (const sc of scenarios) {\n    const wc = Washes.create(makeEl('div'));\n    wc._debug_paintGrid(0.5);\n    wc.splash([{ x: 324, y: 270, velocity: sc.vel }], 'deluge');\n    wc._debug_run(5);\n    const a = wc._debug_anisotropy(100);\n    console.log(`  ${sc.label.padEnd(32)} card=${a.card.toFixed(3)}  diag=${a.diag.toFixed(3)}  ratio=${a.ratio.toFixed(3)}`);\n  }\n}\n\n// ===========================================================================\n// PATTERN 2 \u2014 Per-cell trace over time\n// ===========================================================================\n// This is the test that finally cracked the v0.72 pinpoint. The earlier v0.70\n// and v0.71 fixes (smoothing g/d/wet/pressure at \"skipped\" cells) only made\n// things worse: they created a pressure dip that, after the initial outward\n// injection decayed, reversed neighbors' velocity to point INWARD and turned\n// the skipped cell into a pigment sink. Visible only by tracing velocities\n// across multiple frames \u2014 no single-frame snapshot showed it.\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\nfunction runTrace(libPath) {\n  installMockDOM();\n  const Washes = loadLibWithHelpers(libPath, `\n    _debug_simStep(n) { for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) simStep(); },\n    _debug_cell(x, y) {\n      const i = y * GW + x;\n      return {\n        g: g[0][i] + g[1][i] + g[2][i],\n        d: d[0][i] + d[1][i] + d[2][i],\n        u: u[i], v: v[i],\n        wet: wet[i], pressure: pressure[i],\n      };\n    },\n    _debug_paintGrid(amt) {\n      for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) d[0][i] = amt;\n      setActiveRectFull();\n    },`);\n\n  const wc = Washes.create(makeEl('div'));\n  wc._debug_paintGrid(0.5);\n  // Epicenter at (324.4, 270.7) \u2014 a fractional position that triggered the\n  // pinpoint in v0.70-v0.71. Two cells fall inside the d\u00b2<0.5 skip region\n  // (324, 271) and (325, 271).\n  wc.splash([{ x: 324.4, y: 270.7, velocity: 40 }], 'deluge');\n\n  const cells = [[324, 271], [325, 271], [323, 271], [324, 270]];\n\n  function snapshot(label) {\n    console.log('\\n  ' + label);\n    for (const [x, y] of cells) {\n      const c = wc._debug_cell(x, y);\n      console.log(\n        `    (${x},${y}):  g=${c.g.toFixed(3)} d=${c.d.toFixed(3)} ` +\n        `u=${c.u.toFixed(3).padStart(7)} v=${c.v.toFixed(3).padStart(7)} ` +\n        `wet=${c.wet.toFixed(3)} pres=${c.pressure.toFixed(2)}`\n      );\n    }\n  }\n\n  console.log('\\n=== Per-cell trace (splash at (324.4, 270.7)) ===');\n  console.log('  Watch the u column for the inward (target=324) cells:');\n  console.log('  west (323): u should stay NEGATIVE (outward, away from epicenter)');\n  console.log('  east (325): u should stay POSITIVE (outward, away from epicenter)');\n  console.log('  Sign reversal = pinpoint bug (pre-v0.72).');\n  snapshot('Immediately after splash:');\n  wc._debug_simStep(1);  snapshot('After 1 simStep:');\n  wc._debug_simStep(9);  snapshot('After 10 simSteps:');\n  wc._debug_simStep(20); snapshot('After 30 simSteps:');\n}\n\n// ===========================================================================\n// PATTERN 3 \u2014 Hotspot scan\n// ===========================================================================\n// Sweep the grid for cells that have substantially more pigment than their\n// 4-cardinal neighbors. Used to verify v0.72: no pinpoints near the splash\n// epicenter, only the expected corner-accumulation hotspots from mass-\n// conserving advection at canvas boundaries. The scan is brute-force O(GW*GH)\n// but fast enough for a single test pass.\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\nfunction runHotspots(libPath) {\n  installMockDOM();\n  const Washes = loadLibWithHelpers(libPath, `\n    _debug_simStep(n) { for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) simStep(); },\n    _debug_findHotspots(threshold) {\n      const hotspots = [];\n      for (let y = 2; y < GH - 2; y++) {\n        for (let x = 2; x < GW - 2; x++) {\n          const i = y * GW + x;\n          const self = g[0][i] + g[1][i] + g[2][i] + d[0][i] + d[1][i] + d[2][i];\n          if (self < 0.005) continue;\n          const e = i + 1, w = i - 1, n = i - GW, s = i + GW;\n          const avgN = (\n            (g[0][e]+g[1][e]+g[2][e]+d[0][e]+d[1][e]+d[2][e]) +\n            (g[0][w]+g[1][w]+g[2][w]+d[0][w]+d[1][w]+d[2][w]) +\n            (g[0][n]+g[1][n]+g[2][n]+d[0][n]+d[1][n]+d[2][n]) +\n            (g[0][s]+g[1][s]+g[2][s]+d[0][s]+d[1][s]+d[2][s])\n          ) * 0.25;\n          if (self > avgN * threshold && self - avgN > 0.005) {\n            hotspots.push({ x, y, self: self.toFixed(4), avg: avgN.toFixed(4) });\n          }\n        }\n      }\n      return hotspots;\n    },\n    _debug_paintGrid(amt) {\n      for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) d[0][i] = amt;\n      setActiveRectFull();\n    },`);\n\n  // A spread of fractional epicenter positions. Pre-v0.72, several of these\n  // produced pinpoints near the click site. Post-v0.72, only canvas corners\n  // appear (expected mass-conservation boundary accumulation).\n  const cases = [\n    [324.0, 270.0], [324.1, 270.0], [324.0, 270.1], [324.1, 270.1],\n    [324.3, 270.7], [324.4, 270.7], [324.5, 270.5], [324.6, 270.3],\n    [324.7, 270.7], [324.8, 270.8], [324.9, 270.9],\n    [324.49, 270.51], [324.5, 270.49], [324.45, 270.55],\n  ];\n\n  console.log('\\n=== Hotspot scan over fractional epicenters ===');\n  console.log('  Each row: epicenter coord \u2192 hotspots (self/avg ratio > 1.5\u00d7).');\n  console.log('  Expected (post-v0.72): only canvas corners, no near-epicenter hotspots.');\n  for (const [ex, ey] of cases) {\n    const wc = Washes.create(makeEl('div'));\n    wc._debug_paintGrid(0.5);\n    wc.splash([{ x: ex, y: ey, velocity: 40 }], 'deluge');\n    wc._debug_simStep(30);\n    const hs = wc._debug_findHotspots(1.5);\n    const summary = hs.length === 0\n      ? 'CLEAN'\n      : `${hs.length} hotspot(s): ${hs.slice(0, 3).map((h) => `(${h.x},${h.y})`).join(', ')}`;\n    console.log(`  ec=(${ex}, ${ey})  \u2192  ${summary}`);\n  }\n}\n\n// ===========================================================================\n// PATTERN 4 \u2014 CFL bound check\n// ===========================================================================\n// First-order donor-cell advection is conditionally stable: |u|\u00b7\u0394t + |v|\u00b7\u0394t\n// must be \u2264 1 per cell or the cell donates more than it holds, goes negative,\n// and Kubelka-Munk renders negative values as brighter-than-paper streaks.\n// Useful as an early sanity check when changing velocity scales or timestep.\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\nfunction runCFL(libPath) {\n  installMockDOM();\n  const Washes = loadLibWithHelpers(libPath, `\n    _debug_maxCFL(adt) {\n      let max = 0;\n      for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n        const c = (Math.abs(u[i]) + Math.abs(v[i])) * adt;\n        if (c > max) max = c;\n      }\n      return max;\n    },\n    _debug_paintGrid(amt) {\n      for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) g[0][i] = amt;\n      setActiveRectFull();\n    },`);\n\n  const scenarios = [\n    { vel: 5,  adt: 1, label: 'velocity=5  adt=1' },\n    { vel: 10, adt: 1, label: 'velocity=10 adt=1' },\n    { vel: 40, adt: 1, label: 'velocity=40 adt=1 (default deluge)' },\n    { vel: 40, adt: 0.2941, label: 'velocity=40 adt\u22480.294 (semilag inner)' },\n  ];\n\n  console.log('\\n=== CFL bound (|u|+|v|)\u00b7adt per cell ===');\n  console.log('  Donor-cell stable when max \u2264 1. Semi-Lagrangian has no bound.');\n  for (const sc of scenarios) {\n    const wc = Washes.create(makeEl('div'));\n    wc._debug_paintGrid(0.5);\n    wc.splash([{ x: 324, y: 270, velocity: sc.vel }], 'deluge');\n    const maxCFL = wc._debug_maxCFL(sc.adt);\n    const verdict = maxCFL > 1 ? 'UNSTABLE (donor-cell)' : 'stable';\n    console.log(`  ${sc.label.padEnd(40)} max=${maxCFL.toFixed(2)}  ${verdict}`);\n  }\n}\n\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n// CLI dispatcher\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n\nconst libPath = process.argv[2];\nconst pattern = process.argv[3] || 'all';\n\nif (!libPath) {\n  console.error('usage: node washes-test-harness.js <path-to-watercolor-lib.js> [pattern]');\n  console.error('patterns: anisotropy | trace | hotspots | cfl | all');\n  process.exit(1);\n}\nif (!fs.existsSync(libPath)) {\n  console.error('lib not found:', libPath);\n  process.exit(1);\n}\n\nconst patterns = {\n  anisotropy: runAnisotropy,\n  trace: runTrace,\n  hotspots: runHotspots,\n  cfl: runCFL,\n};\n\nif (pattern === 'all') {\n  for (const name of Object.keys(patterns)) patterns[name](libPath);\n} else if (patterns[pattern]) {\n  patterns[pattern](libPath);\n} else {\n  console.error('unknown pattern:', pattern);\n  console.error('available:', Object.keys(patterns).join(', '));\n  process.exit(1);\n}\n",

    "display_content": {
      "type": "json_block",
      "json_block": "{\"language\": \"javascript\", \"code\": \"// washes-test-harness.js\\n//\\n// Headless Node.js test harness for the Washes watercolor lib,\\n// extracted from the diagnostic scripts that drove the v0.56-v0.72\\n// bug-hunting arc.\\n//\\n// The lib targets browsers but its core simulation is pure JS arrays\\n// and math. This harness stubs the DOM/Canvas APIs so the lib can be\\n// loaded in Node, then injects per-test `_debug_*` helpers via string\\n// replacement before eval'ing the source. The result: a few seconds\\n// of numerical inspection instead of staring at rendered output for\\n// 13 versions.\\n//\\n// USAGE:\\n//   node washes-test-harness.js  /path/to/watercolor-lib.js  [pattern]\\n//\\n// PATTERNS:\\n//   anisotropy     \\u2014 cardinal vs diagonal advection ratio (v0.69 \\u221a2 bug)\\n//   trace          \\u2014 single-cell state over many frames  (v0.72 pinpoint)\\n//   hotspots       \\u2014 scan grid for cells differing significantly from\\n//                    their neighbors (v0.71-0.72 verification)\\n//   cfl            \\u2014 CFL stability bound for explicit advection\\n//   all            \\u2014 run all patterns\\n//\\n// EXAMPLE:\\n//   node washes-test-harness.js ./watercolor-lib.js anisotropy\\n//\\n// This file is meant to be copied and adapted \\u2014 each pattern below\\n// is a self-contained example of the diagnostic technique it was\\n// named after.\\n\\n'use strict';\\n\\nconst fs = require('fs');\\n\\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n// Mock DOM. Just enough to get window.Washes.create() to run. None of these\\n// stubs do anything useful visually \\u2014 they just satisfy the lib's bootstrap\\n// code that touches document/window/canvas APIs.\\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n\\nfunction makeEl(tag) {\\n  const e = {\\n    tagName: (tag || 'div').toUpperCase(),\\n    nodeName: (tag || 'div').toUpperCase(),\\n    attributes: {},\\n    children: [],\\n    childNodes: [],\\n    style: {},\\n    dataset: {},\\n    parentNode: null,\\n    parentElement: null,\\n    width: 1024,\\n    height: 768,\\n    _listeners: {},\\n    ownerSVGElement: null,\\n    classList: {\\n      toggle() {}, add() {}, remove() {}, contains() { return false; },\\n    },\\n    setAttribute(n, v) { this.attributes[n] = String(v); },\\n    getAttribute(n) {\\n      return Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(this.attributes, n)\\n        ? this.attributes[n] : null;\\n    },\\n    appendChild(c) {\\n      this.children.push(c);\\n      this.childNodes.push(c);\\n      c.parentNode = this;\\n      c.parentElement = this;\\n      return c;\\n    },\\n    removeChild(c) {\\n      this.children = this.children.filter((x) => x !== c);\\n      return c;\\n    },\\n    replaceChildren() {\\n      this.children = [];\\n      this.childNodes = [];\\n    },\\n    addEventListener(t, fn) {\\n      this._listeners[t] = this._listeners[t] || [];\\n      this._listeners[t].push(fn);\\n    },\\n    removeEventListener() {},\\n    dispatchEvent(ev) {\\n      (this._listeners[ev.type] || []).forEach((f) => f(ev));\\n      return true;\\n    },\\n    setPointerCapture() {},\\n    releasePointerCapture() {},\\n    getBoundingClientRect() {\\n      return { left: 0, top: 0, right: 1080, bottom: 900, width: 1080, height: 900, x: 0, y: 0 };\\n    },\\n    toDataURL() { return 'data:image/png;base64,F'; },\\n    toBlob(cb) { setTimeout(() => cb({ size: 1, type: 'image/png' }), 0); },\\n    querySelector() { return null; },\\n    querySelectorAll() { return []; },\\n    getContext(t) {\\n      if (t === 'webgl2') return null;\\n      return {\\n        createImageData: (w, h) => ({ data: new Uint8ClampedArray(w * h * 4), width: w, height: h }),\\n        getImageData: (x, y, w, h) => ({ data: new Uint8ClampedArray(w * h * 4), width: w, height: h }),\\n        putImageData() {}, drawImage() {}, clearRect() {}, fillRect() {}, fillText() {},\\n        measureText() { return { width: 50 }; },\\n        save() {}, restore() {}, translate() {}, rotate() {}, scale() {},\\n        imageSmoothingEnabled: true, imageSmoothingQuality: 'high',\\n        fillStyle: '', strokeStyle: '', font: '',\\n        textBaseline: '', textAlign: '',\\n        globalAlpha: 1, globalCompositeOperation: 'source-over',\\n      };\\n    },\\n  };\\n  return e;\\n}\\n\\nfunction installMockDOM() {\\n  global.document = {\\n    _body: makeEl('body'),\\n    createElement: (t) => makeEl(t),\\n    createElementNS: (ns, t) => makeEl(t),\\n    getElementById: () => null,\\n    querySelectorAll: () => [],\\n    documentElement: { style: { setProperty() {} }, dataset: {} },\\n  };\\n  global.document.body = global.document._body;\\n  global.window = {\\n    innerWidth: 1080,\\n    innerHeight: 900,\\n    devicePixelRatio: 1,\\n    addEventListener() {},\\n    location: { search: '' },\\n    requestAnimationFrame: () => 0,\\n    cancelAnimationFrame: () => {},\\n    matchMedia: () => ({ matches: false }),\\n  };\\n  global.navigator = { maxTouchPoints: 0 };\\n  global.requestAnimationFrame = () => 0;\\n  global.cancelAnimationFrame = () => {};\\n  global.performance = { now: () => Date.now() };\\n  global.URLSearchParams = URLSearchParams;\\n  global.URL = { createObjectURL: () => 'blob:fake', revokeObjectURL: () => {} };\\n  global.Blob = function () {};\\n  global.DOMParser = function () {\\n    return { parseFromString: () => ({ querySelector: () => null, querySelectorAll: () => [] }) };\\n  };\\n  global.Image = function () {};\\n  global.CustomEvent = function (t, o) { this.type = t; this.detail = o && o.detail; };\\n}\\n\\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n// Load the lib with debug helpers injected. The trick: inject `_debug_*`\\n// methods into the public API object via string replacement on the source,\\n// then eval. This avoids modifying the lib file on disk and gives each test\\n// scoped access to internal state (g, d, u, v, wet, pressure, etc.).\\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n\\nfunction loadLibWithHelpers(libPath, extraMethods) {\\n  const src = fs.readFileSync(libPath, 'utf8');\\n  const patched = src.replace('  state() {', extraMethods + '\\\\n  state() {');\\n  new Function(patched)();\\n  return global.window.Washes;\\n}\\n\\n// ===========================================================================\\n// PATTERN 1 \\u2014 Anisotropy detection\\n// ===========================================================================\\n// This is the test that found the v0.69 cross-artifact root cause. After 13\\n// versions of patching the pigment advection code (donor-cell, flux clamp,\\n// substep CFL, semi-Lagrangian, mass-conserving semi-Lagrangian), the cross\\n// still appeared. Comparing pigment density at distance d in cardinal vs\\n// diagonal directions revealed a ratio of \\u221a2 \\u2014 the tell of an L1-vs-L2 norm\\n// confusion. Root cause: per-axis velocity clamp instead of magnitude clamp.\\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n\\nfunction runAnisotropy(libPath) {\\n  installMockDOM();\\n  const Washes = loadLibWithHelpers(libPath, `\\n    _debug_run(n) {\\n      for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) { movePigment(); transferPigment(); }\\n    },\\n    _debug_anisotropy(d) {\\n      const cx = Math.floor(GW / 2), cy = Math.floor(GH / 2);\\n      const dd = Math.round(d / Math.SQRT2);\\n      function get(x, y) {\\n        return g[0][y * GW + x] + g[1][y * GW + x] + g[2][y * GW + x];\\n      }\\n      const card = (get(cx + d, cy) + get(cx - d, cy) + get(cx, cy - d) + get(cx, cy + d)) / 4;\\n      const diag = (get(cx + dd, cy - dd) + get(cx - dd, cy - dd) + get(cx + dd, cy + dd) + get(cx - dd, cy + dd)) / 4;\\n      return { card, diag, ratio: diag > 0 ? card / diag : Infinity };\\n    },\\n    _debug_paintGrid(amt) {\\n      for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) g[0][i] = amt;\\n      setActiveRectFull();\\n    },`);\\n\\n  const scenarios = [\\n    { vel: 40, label: 'velocity=40 (default deluge)' },\\n    { vel: 10, label: 'velocity=10 (CFL safe)' },\\n    { vel: 5,  label: 'velocity=5  (well below CFL)' },\\n  ];\\n\\n  console.log('\\\\n=== Anisotropy (cardinal/diagonal ratio at d=100) ===');\\n  console.log('  ratio = 1.0 is isotropic; ~1.4 = \\u221a2 = per-axis L1 norm bug');\\n  for (const sc of scenarios) {\\n    const wc = Washes.create(makeEl('div'));\\n    wc._debug_paintGrid(0.5);\\n    wc.splash([{ x: 324, y: 270, velocity: sc.vel }], 'deluge');\\n    wc._debug_run(5);\\n    const a = wc._debug_anisotropy(100);\\n    console.log(`  ${sc.label.padEnd(32)} card=${a.card.toFixed(3)}  diag=${a.diag.toFixed(3)}  ratio=${a.ratio.toFixed(3)}`);\\n  }\\n}\\n\\n// ===========================================================================\\n// PATTERN 2 \\u2014 Per-cell trace over time\\n// ===========================================================================\\n// This is the test that finally cracked the v0.72 pinpoint. The earlier v0.70\\n// and v0.71 fixes (smoothing g/d/wet/pressure at \\\"skipped\\\" cells) only made\\n// things worse: they created a pressure dip that, after the initial outward\\n// injection decayed, reversed neighbors' velocity to point INWARD and turned\\n// the skipped cell into a pigment sink. Visible only by tracing velocities\\n// across multiple frames \\u2014 no single-frame snapshot showed it.\\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n\\nfunction runTrace(libPath) {\\n  installMockDOM();\\n  const Washes = loadLibWithHelpers(libPath, `\\n    _debug_simStep(n) { for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) simStep(); },\\n    _debug_cell(x, y) {\\n      const i = y * GW + x;\\n      return {\\n        g: g[0][i] + g[1][i] + g[2][i],\\n        d: d[0][i] + d[1][i] + d[2][i],\\n        u: u[i], v: v[i],\\n        wet: wet[i], pressure: pressure[i],\\n      };\\n    },\\n    _debug_paintGrid(amt) {\\n      for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) d[0][i] = amt;\\n      setActiveRectFull();\\n    },`);\\n\\n  const wc = Washes.create(makeEl('div'));\\n  wc._debug_paintGrid(0.5);\\n  // Epicenter at (324.4, 270.7) \\u2014 a fractional position that triggered the\\n  // pinpoint in v0.70-v0.71. Two cells fall inside the d\\u00b2<0.5 skip region\\n  // (324, 271) and (325, 271).\\n  wc.splash([{ x: 324.4, y: 270.7, velocity: 40 }], 'deluge');\\n\\n  const cells = [[324, 271], [325, 271], [323, 271], [324, 270]];\\n\\n  function snapshot(label) {\\n    console.log('\\\\n  ' + label);\\n    for (const [x, y] of cells) {\\n      const c = wc._debug_cell(x, y);\\n      console.log(\\n        `    (${x},${y}):  g=${c.g.toFixed(3)} d=${c.d.toFixed(3)} ` +\\n        `u=${c.u.toFixed(3).padStart(7)} v=${c.v.toFixed(3).padStart(7)} ` +\\n        `wet=${c.wet.toFixed(3)} pres=${c.pressure.toFixed(2)}`\\n      );\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  console.log('\\\\n=== Per-cell trace (splash at (324.4, 270.7)) ===');\\n  console.log('  Watch the u column for the inward (target=324) cells:');\\n  console.log('  west (323): u should stay NEGATIVE (outward, away from epicenter)');\\n  console.log('  east (325): u should stay POSITIVE (outward, away from epicenter)');\\n  console.log('  Sign reversal = pinpoint bug (pre-v0.72).');\\n  snapshot('Immediately after splash:');\\n  wc._debug_simStep(1);  snapshot('After 1 simStep:');\\n  wc._debug_simStep(9);  snapshot('After 10 simSteps:');\\n  wc._debug_simStep(20); snapshot('After 30 simSteps:');\\n}\\n\\n// ===========================================================================\\n// PATTERN 3 \\u2014 Hotspot scan\\n// ===========================================================================\\n// Sweep the grid for cells that have substantially more pigment than their\\n// 4-cardinal neighbors. Used to verify v0.72: no pinpoints near the splash\\n// epicenter, only the expected corner-accumulation hotspots from mass-\\n// conserving advection at canvas boundaries. The scan is brute-force O(GW*GH)\\n// but fast enough for a single test pass.\\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n\\nfunction runHotspots(libPath) {\\n  installMockDOM();\\n  const Washes = loadLibWithHelpers(libPath, `\\n    _debug_simStep(n) { for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) simStep(); },\\n    _debug_findHotspots(threshold) {\\n      const hotspots = [];\\n      for (let y = 2; y < GH - 2; y++) {\\n        for (let x = 2; x < GW - 2; x++) {\\n          const i = y * GW + x;\\n          const self = g[0][i] + g[1][i] + g[2][i] + d[0][i] + d[1][i] + d[2][i];\\n          if (self < 0.005) continue;\\n          const e = i + 1, w = i - 1, n = i - GW, s = i + GW;\\n          const avgN = (\\n            (g[0][e]+g[1][e]+g[2][e]+d[0][e]+d[1][e]+d[2][e]) +\\n            (g[0][w]+g[1][w]+g[2][w]+d[0][w]+d[1][w]+d[2][w]) +\\n            (g[0][n]+g[1][n]+g[2][n]+d[0][n]+d[1][n]+d[2][n]) +\\n            (g[0][s]+g[1][s]+g[2][s]+d[0][s]+d[1][s]+d[2][s])\\n          ) * 0.25;\\n          if (self > avgN * threshold && self - avgN > 0.005) {\\n            hotspots.push({ x, y, self: self.toFixed(4), avg: avgN.toFixed(4) });\\n          }\\n        }\\n      }\\n      return hotspots;\\n    },\\n    _debug_paintGrid(amt) {\\n      for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) d[0][i] = amt;\\n      setActiveRectFull();\\n    },`);\\n\\n  // A spread of fractional epicenter positions. Pre-v0.72, several of these\\n  // produced pinpoints near the click site. Post-v0.72, only canvas corners\\n  // appear (expected mass-conservation boundary accumulation).\\n  const cases = [\\n    [324.0, 270.0], [324.1, 270.0], [324.0, 270.1], [324.1, 270.1],\\n    [324.3, 270.7], [324.4, 270.7], [324.5, 270.5], [324.6, 270.3],\\n    [324.7, 270.7], [324.8, 270.8], [324.9, 270.9],\\n    [324.49, 270.51], [324.5, 270.49], [324.45, 270.55],\\n  ];\\n\\n  console.log('\\\\n=== Hotspot scan over fractional epicenters ===');\\n  console.log('  Each row: epicenter coord \\u2192 hotspots (self/avg ratio > 1.5\\u00d7).');\\n  console.log('  Expected (post-v0.72): only canvas corners, no near-epicenter hotspots.');\\n  for (const [ex, ey] of cases) {\\n    const wc = Washes.create(makeEl('div'));\\n    wc._debug_paintGrid(0.5);\\n    wc.splash([{ x: ex, y: ey, velocity: 40 }], 'deluge');\\n    wc._debug_simStep(30);\\n    const hs = wc._debug_findHotspots(1.5);\\n    const summary = hs.length === 0\\n      ? 'CLEAN'\\n      : `${hs.length} hotspot(s): ${hs.slice(0, 3).map((h) => `(${h.x},${h.y})`).join(', ')}`;\\n    console.log(`  ec=(${ex}, ${ey})  \\u2192  ${summary}`);\\n  }\\n}\\n\\n// ===========================================================================\\n// PATTERN 4 \\u2014 CFL bound check\\n// ===========================================================================\\n// First-order donor-cell advection is conditionally stable: |u|\\u00b7\\u0394t + |v|\\u00b7\\u0394t\\n// must be \\u2264 1 per cell or the cell donates more than it holds, goes negative,\\n// and Kubelka-Munk renders negative values as brighter-than-paper streaks.\\n// Useful as an early sanity check when changing velocity scales or timestep.\\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n\\nfunction runCFL(libPath) {\\n  installMockDOM();\\n  const Washes = loadLibWithHelpers(libPath, `\\n    _debug_maxCFL(adt) {\\n      let max = 0;\\n      for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\\n        const c = (Math.abs(u[i]) + Math.abs(v[i])) * adt;\\n        if (c > max) max = c;\\n      }\\n      return max;\\n    },\\n    _debug_paintGrid(amt) {\\n      for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) g[0][i] = amt;\\n      setActiveRectFull();\\n    },`);\\n\\n  const scenarios = [\\n    { vel: 5,  adt: 1, label: 'velocity=5  adt=1' },\\n    { vel: 10, adt: 1, label: 'velocity=10 adt=1' },\\n    { vel: 40, adt: 1, label: 'velocity=40 adt=1 (default deluge)' },\\n    { vel: 40, adt: 0.2941, label: 'velocity=40 adt\\u22480.294 (semilag inner)' },\\n  ];\\n\\n  console.log('\\\\n=== CFL bound (|u|+|v|)\\u00b7adt per cell ===');\\n  console.log('  Donor-cell stable when max \\u2264 1. Semi-Lagrangian has no bound.');\\n  for (const sc of scenarios) {\\n    const wc = Washes.create(makeEl('div'));\\n    wc._debug_paintGrid(0.5);\\n    wc.splash([{ x: 324, y: 270, velocity: sc.vel }], 'deluge');\\n    const maxCFL = wc._debug_maxCFL(sc.adt);\\n    const verdict = maxCFL > 1 ? 'UNSTABLE (donor-cell)' : 'stable';\\n    console.log(`  ${sc.label.padEnd(40)} max=${maxCFL.toFixed(2)}  ${verdict}`);\\n  }\\n}\\n\\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n// CLI dispatcher\\n// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n\\nconst libPath = process.argv[2];\\nconst pattern = process.argv[3] || 'all';\\n\\nif (!libPath) {\\n  console.error('usage: node washes-test-harness.js <path-to-watercolor-lib.js> [pattern]');\\n  console.error('patterns: anisotropy | trace | hotspots | cfl | all');\\n  process.exit(1);\\n}\\nif (!fs.existsSync(libPath)) {\\n  console.error('lib not found:', libPath);\\n  process.exit(1);\\n}\\n\\nconst patterns = {\\n  anisotropy: runAnisotropy,\\n  trace: runTrace,\\n  hotspots: runHotspots,\\n  cfl: runCFL,\\n};\\n\\nif (pattern === 'all') {\\n  for (const name of Object.keys(patterns)) patterns[name](libPath);\\n} else if (patterns[pattern]) {\\n  patterns[pattern](libPath);\\n} else {\\n  console.error('unknown pattern:', pattern);\\n  console.error('available:', Object.keys(patterns).join(', '));\\n  process.exit(1);\\n}\\n\", \"filename\": \"/home/claude/washes-test-harness.js\"}"

    "input": {
      "description": "Use defineProperty for navigator and other read-only globals on modern Node",
      "new_str": "  global.document.body = global.document._body;\n  global.window = {\n    innerWidth: 1080,\n    innerHeight: 900,\n    devicePixelRatio: 1,\n    addEventListener() {},\n    location: { search: '' },\n    requestAnimationFrame: () => 0,\n    cancelAnimationFrame: () => {},\n    matchMedia: () => ({ matches: false }),\n  };\n  // navigator and performance became read-only getters on global in Node 20+;\n  // defineProperty works where direct assignment doesn't.\n  Object.defineProperty(global, 'navigator', {\n    value: { maxTouchPoints: 0 },\n    configurable: true, writable: true,\n  });\n  Object.defineProperty(global, 'performance', {\n    value: { now: () => Date.now() },\n    configurable: true, writable: true,\n  });\n  global.requestAnimationFrame = () => 0;\n  global.cancelAnimationFrame = () => {};",

ec=(324, 270.1)  \u2192  4 hotspot(s): (2,2), (645,2), (2,537)\\n  ec=(324.1, 270.1)  \u2192  4 hotspot(s): (2,2), (645,2), (2,537)\\n  ec=(324.3, 270.7)  \u2192  4 hotspot(s): (2,2), (645,2), (2,537)\\n  ec=(324.4, 270.7)  \u2192  4 hotspot(s): (2,2), (645,2), (2,537)\\n  ec=(324.5, 270.5)  \u2192  4 hotspot(s): (2,2), (645,2), (2,537)\\n  ec=(324.6, 270.3)  \u2192  4 hotspot(s): (2,2), (645,2), (2,537)\\n  ec=(324.7, 270.7)  \u2192  4 hotspot(s): (2,2), (645,2), (2,537)\\n  ec=(324.8, 270.8)  \u2192  4 hotspot(s): (2,2), (645,2), (2,537)\\n\",\"stderr\":\"\"}"

    "text": "All four patterns run cleanly and confirm v0.72's fixes:\n\n- **Anisotropy**: ratio \u2248 1.0 across all velocity scenarios (was \u221a2 \u2248 1.42 pre-v0.69)\n- **Trace**: at the singular cell (324, 271), u stays consistently negative across all 30 frames \u2014 no sign reversal (was +0.573 by frame 10 pre-v0.72)\n- **Hotspot scan**: every case shows only canvas-corner hotspots, no near-epicenter pinpoints\n\nNow add a \"For maintainers\" section to the docs covering the testing patterns, evidence trail, and best practices.       "old_str": "        <h2 id=\"dig-further-reading\">Further reading</h2>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>For more on the K-M model and its limitations on dyed/highly-absorbing papers, see <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kubelka%E2%80%93Munk_theory\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">the Wikipedia K-M article</a> (a good overview) and Westin's translation of the original 1931 paper <a href=\"https://www.graphics.cornell.edu/~westin/pubs/kubelka.pdf\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">[2]</a>.</li>\n          <li>For more on semi-Lagrangian and the broader fluid-solver landscape, the canonical modern textbook is Bridson <a href=\"#references\" class=\"docs-cite-ref\">[5]</a>. Stam's \"Stable Fluids\" paper <a href=\"#references\" class=\"docs-cite-ref\">[4]</a> is short, readable, and remains the simplest introduction to the technique. Dan Morris has an even-more-introductory annotated walkthrough at <a href=\"https://dmorris.net/projects/summaries/dmorris.stable_fluids.notes.pdf\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">dmorris.net</a>.</li>\n          <li>For more on Curtis et al.'s shallow-water approach in particular, the SIGGRAPH '97 paper <a href=\"#references\" class=\"docs-cite-ref\">[1]</a> is the source. Yann Semet's <a href=\"https://www.slideshare.net/ysemet/computer-generated-watercolor-1858806\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">slide presentation</a> gives a good high-level overview.</li>\n          <li>For more on the CFL condition and its modern descendents (von Neumann analysis, Lax equivalence), <em>The Courant-Friedrichs-Lewy (CFL) Condition: 80 Years After Its Discovery</em> (Kubrusly &amp; de Moura, eds., Birkh\u00e4user 2012) collects current applications and historical perspective.</li>\n        </ul>\n      </section>",
      "path": "/home/claude/watercolor.html"
    },

    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-13T02:48:23.313764Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-13T02:49:26.725787Z",
