    "input": {
      "description": "Restore Further Reading and add new For Maintainers section after it",
      "new_str": "        <h2 id=\"dig-further-reading\">Further reading</h2>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>For more on the K-M model and its limitations on dyed/highly-absorbing papers, see <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kubelka%E2%80%93Munk_theory\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">the Wikipedia K-M article</a> (a good overview) and Westin's translation of the original 1931 paper <a href=\"https://www.graphics.cornell.edu/~westin/pubs/kubelka.pdf\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">[2]</a>.</li>\n          <li>For more on semi-Lagrangian and the broader fluid-solver landscape, the canonical modern textbook is Bridson <a href=\"#references\" class=\"docs-cite-ref\">[5]</a>. Stam's \"Stable Fluids\" paper <a href=\"#references\" class=\"docs-cite-ref\">[4]</a> is short, readable, and remains the simplest introduction to the technique. Dan Morris has an even-more-introductory annotated walkthrough at <a href=\"https://dmorris.net/projects/summaries/dmorris.stable_fluids.notes.pdf\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">dmorris.net</a>.</li>\n          <li>For more on Curtis et al.'s shallow-water approach in particular, the SIGGRAPH '97 paper <a href=\"#references\" class=\"docs-cite-ref\">[1]</a> is the source. Yann Semet's <a href=\"https://www.slideshare.net/ysemet/computer-generated-watercolor-1858806\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">slide presentation</a> gives a good high-level overview.</li>\n          <li>For more on the CFL condition and its modern descendents (von Neumann analysis, Lax equivalence), <em>The Courant-Friedrichs-Lewy (CFL) Condition: 80 Years After Its Discovery</em> (Kubrusly &amp; de Moura, eds., Birkh\u00e4user 2012) collects current applications and historical perspective.</li>\n        </ul>\n      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== FOR MAINTAINERS ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\" data-section=\"for-maintainers\">\n        <h1 id=\"for-maintainers\">For maintainers</h1>\n        <p class=\"docs-lede\">\n          Tools and patterns from the v0.56-v0.72 development arc &mdash;\n          useful if you ever need to debug a regression, port the lib\n          somewhere new, or extend it. Most of this content emerged\n          from chasing two specific artifacts (the cross and the\n          pinpoint) across 17 versions. A companion file,\n          <code>washes-test-harness.js</code>, ships alongside this\n          page and runs all the diagnostic patterns described below.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"maint-evidence\">Evidence trail: where each bug was first spotted</h2>\n        <p>\n          The cross and pinpoint artifacts were diagnosed iteratively\n          across many user-reported observations. Below is the\n          timeline of what was reported, what was tested in response,\n          and what the result drove.\n        </p>\n        <ol class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>\n            <strong>v0.61-0.62 \u2014 screen recording of the cross during a deluge.</strong>\n            User showed a deluge wavefront leaving four arms in\n            cardinal directions, mostly visible above CFL bound\n            (velocity = 40). Initial hypothesis: donor-cell advection\n            anisotropy at high CFL. Drove v0.65 (substep advection).\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>v0.66-0.68 \u2014 cross persists in screenshots across all four advection modes.</strong>\n            Tried donor-cell, flux-clamp, substep CFL-safe,\n            semi-Lagrangian. Cross visible in every mode. This was\n            the key signal: <em>the artifact is not in the pigment\n            advection code</em>. Drove anisotropy testing in v0.69.\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>v0.69 \u2014 numerical confirmation of \u221a2 anisotropy in the velocity field itself.</strong>\n            Running <code>_debug_anisotropy</code> 5 steps after a\n            splash: cardinal velocity magnitude 1.14, diagonal\n            magnitude 1.62. Ratio 1.42 \u2248 \u221a2. The L1-vs-L2 norm tell.\n            Root cause: per-axis velocity clamp making the allowed\n            region a square. Fixed in v0.69 with magnitude clamp.\n            Cross was gone.\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>v0.69-0.70 \u2014 screenshot of a single dark pixel at the center of cleared paper.</strong>\n            New artifact: a pinpoint at the splash epicenter when\n            triggered by the \"Deluge Now\" button (integer-valued ec).\n            Diagnosed: the <code>d\u00b2 &lt; 0.5</code> injection guard\n            left the epicenter cell singular (u=v=0, p=0). Patched\n            in v0.70 with post-process spatial smoothing of one cell.\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>v0.71 \u2014 user report: \"every other click\" still pinpoints.</strong>\n            v0.70 only handled the integer-ec case. For\n            offset-fractional ec coordinates (e.g. (324.4, 270.7)),\n            two cells fell inside the d\u00b2&lt;0.5 region, not one.\n            Generalized the smoothing to scan all 4 candidates in the\n            2\u00d72 grid around the ec.\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>v0.72 \u2014 user report: \"every three clicks\" still pinpoints.</strong>\n            v0.71 reduced frequency but didn't eliminate. Running\n            <code>_debug_cell</code> over 30 frames revealed\n            something unexpected: by frame 10, neighbors' velocities\n            had reversed direction and were pointing INTO the singular\n            cell. The smoothing of pressure had created a pressure dip;\n            once the initial outward injection decayed, the dip became\n            the dominant gradient and pulled flow inward. <em>The\n            smoothing fix was creating worse pinpoints than the\n            original singularity.</em> Real root cause: the\n            <code>d\u00b2&lt;0.5</code> guard itself. Tightened to\n            <code>d\u00b2&lt;1e-10</code>; pinpoints gone everywhere.\n          </li>\n        </ol>\n\n        <h2 id=\"maint-harness\">Headless test harness</h2>\n        <p>\n          The lib targets browsers, but its core simulation is pure\n          JS arrays and math. To inspect numerical state without a\n          rendering loop, stub the DOM/Canvas APIs and load the lib\n          in Node. This is what every diagnostic in the v0.56-0.72\n          arc used.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          The stub doesn't need to do anything useful visually\n          &mdash; it just needs to satisfy the lib's bootstrap code\n          that touches <code>document.createElement</code>,\n          <code>window.innerWidth</code>, <code>canvas.getContext</code>,\n          and similar. Once the stubs are in place,\n          <code>new Function(libSource)()</code> runs the lib and\n          exposes <code>window.Washes</code>. A full working stub is\n          in <code>washes-test-harness.js</code>; the essential parts\n          look like this:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// Minimal mock DOM. Stub everything the lib touches.\nfunction makeEl() {\n  return {\n    style: {}, dataset: {}, attributes: {}, children: [],\n    setAttribute(n, v) { this.attributes[n] = v; },\n    appendChild(c) { this.children.push(c); return c; },\n    addEventListener() {},\n    getBoundingClientRect() {\n      return { width: 1080, height: 900, left: 0, top: 0 };\n    },\n    getContext() { return { /* canvas2d API stubs */ }; },\n  };\n}\nglobal.document = {\n  createElement: makeEl, createElementNS: makeEl,\n  getElementById: () => null, body: makeEl(),\n  documentElement: { style: { setProperty() {} } },\n};\nglobal.window = { innerWidth: 1080, innerHeight: 900,\n                  devicePixelRatio: 1, addEventListener() {} };\n// Modern Node: navigator and performance are read-only getters,\n// so use defineProperty instead of direct assignment.\nObject.defineProperty(global, 'navigator', {\n  value: { maxTouchPoints: 0 }, configurable: true, writable: true });\nObject.defineProperty(global, 'performance', {\n  value: { now: () => Date.now() }, configurable: true, writable: true });\n\n// Load the lib.\nconst src = require('fs').readFileSync('./watercolor-lib.js', 'utf8');\nnew Function(src)();\nconst wc = window.Washes.create(makeEl());</pre>\n\n        <h2 id=\"maint-instrument\">Instrumenting the lib (without modifying it)</h2>\n        <p>\n          The lib's public API doesn't expose internal arrays. To\n          inspect them, inject debug methods via string replacement\n          on the source before <code>eval</code>. Find a known\n          insertion point in the public-API object literal and add\n          methods with the same closure scope as\n          <code>state()</code>:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">let src = fs.readFileSync('./watercolor-lib.js', 'utf8');\nsrc = src.replace('  state() {', `\n  _debug_cell(x, y) {\n    const i = y * GW + x;\n    return { g: g[0][i] + g[1][i] + g[2][i],\n             d: d[0][i] + d[1][i] + d[2][i],\n             u: u[i], v: v[i], wet: wet[i], pressure: pressure[i] };\n  },\n  _debug_simStep(n) { for (let i = 0; i &lt; n; i++) simStep(); },\n  _debug_paintGrid(amt) {\n    for (let i = 0; i &lt; N; i++) d[0][i] = amt;\n    setActiveRectFull();\n  },\n  state() {`);\nnew Function(src)();</pre>\n        <p>\n          This adds <code>_debug_*</code> methods that have access\n          to the lib's private state (<code>g</code>, <code>d</code>,\n          <code>u</code>, <code>v</code>, <code>wet</code>,\n          <code>pressure</code>, <code>GW</code>, <code>GH</code>,\n          <code>N</code>) without committing any changes to the lib\n          itself. Each test injects only what it needs.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"maint-patterns\">Four diagnostic patterns</h2>\n        <p>\n          Each pattern below is implemented in\n          <code>washes-test-harness.js</code> and is named for the\n          bug it caught. Run with:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">node washes-test-harness.js ./watercolor-lib.js anisotropy\nnode washes-test-harness.js ./watercolor-lib.js trace\nnode washes-test-harness.js ./watercolor-lib.js hotspots\nnode washes-test-harness.js ./watercolor-lib.js cfl\nnode washes-test-harness.js ./watercolor-lib.js all</pre>\n\n        <h3>Pattern 1: anisotropy detection (caught the v0.69 cross)</h3>\n        <p>\n          Compare pigment density at distance <em>d</em> from the\n          splash center in cardinal vs diagonal directions. For an\n          isotropic flow, the ratio should be 1.0. Ratios near \u221a2 or\n          1/\u221a2 are diagnostic of per-axis vs L2-magnitude\n          confusion. Output:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">=== Anisotropy (cardinal/diagonal ratio at d=100) ===\n  velocity=40 (default deluge)     card=0.268  diag=0.269  ratio=0.997\n  velocity=10 (CFL safe)           card=0.423  diag=0.424  ratio=0.999\n  velocity=5  (well below CFL)     card=0.447  diag=0.447  ratio=1.001</pre>\n        <p>\n          Post-v0.69 the ratio is ~1.0 across all velocity scales.\n          Pre-v0.69 at velocity=40 the ratio was ~0.71 (cardinal less\n          than diagonal by \u221a2).\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>Pattern 2: per-cell trace (caught the v0.72 pinpoint)</h3>\n        <p>\n          For one cell of interest, dump <em>(g, d, u, v, wet, p)</em>\n          immediately after the splash and then at frames 1, 10, 30.\n          Look for sign reversals, magnitude inversions, or\n          accumulation. The v0.72 pinpoint was diagnosed via this\n          pattern when neighbors' velocities reversed direction by\n          frame 10. Output (post-v0.72, correct behavior):\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">After 10 simSteps:\n    (324,271):  g=0.010 d=0.020 u= -0.407 v=  0.320  \u2190 target\n    (325,271):  g=0.010 d=0.019 u=  0.501 v=  0.298  \u2190 east, still + (outward)\n    (323,271):  g=0.017 d=0.026 u= -0.917 v=  0.209  \u2190 west, still \u2212 (outward)\n    (324,270):  g=0.011 d=0.018 u= -0.364 v= -0.527  \u2190 north, still \u2212 (outward)</pre>\n        <p>\n          Pre-v0.72 the same cells showed reversed signs at frame 10,\n          and the target cell had accumulated 5\u00d7 the surrounding\n          density by frame 30.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>Pattern 3: hotspot scan (regression check)</h3>\n        <p>\n          Brute-force scan: for every cell, compare its total\n          pigment to the mean of its 4 cardinal neighbors. Cells with\n          self &gt; 1.5\u00d7 neighbor mean (and a meaningful absolute\n          excess) are reported as hotspots. Run with many fractional\n          epicenter coordinates to catch position-dependent\n          regressions. Post-v0.72 output shows only the four canvas\n          corners (expected mass-conservation accumulation at\n          boundaries), with no near-epicenter pinpoints across any\n          tested fractional position.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>Pattern 4: CFL bound check (sanity check before changing scales)</h3>\n        <p>\n          For first-order donor-cell advection on a unit grid, the\n          stability condition is <code>(|u| + |v|)\u00b7\u0394t \u2264 1</code> per\n          cell. Computing the max over the active region tells you\n          whether the velocity scale + timestep combination will\n          stay stable. Useful when changing\n          <code>VEL_CLAMP</code>, <code>DT</code>, or splash velocity\n          defaults. Semi-Lagrangian has no CFL bound, so this check\n          is informational rather than required when running in\n          semilag mode.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"maint-bestpractices\">Best practices for fluid-sim debugging</h2>\n        <p>\n          Distilled from the v0.56-v0.72 arc, in roughly the order I\n          should have applied each.\n        </p>\n        <ol class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>\n            <strong>Symptoms can be inherited from upstream subsystems.</strong>\n            The cross looked exactly like donor-cell axis-aligned\n            error, so I lived in the pigment-advection code for 13\n            versions. The actual source was the velocity clamp one\n            function upstream. <em>Lesson: when an artifact has a\n            strong visual signature, that signature identifies a\n            class of bug, not the specific subsystem producing it.</em>\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>Dump numerical state, don't just look at renders.</strong>\n            The \u221a2 cross diagnosis took 13 versions visually but\n            would have taken 5 minutes if I'd printed\n            <code>{cardinal_speed, diagonal_speed}</code> after one\n            step. Rendered output is the convolution of every layer\n            of the sim; numerical dumps isolate the layers.\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>The \u221a2 (or 1/\u221a2) ratio is a diagnostic tell.</strong>\n            Any 1.4\u00d7 ratio between similar measurements suggests L1\n            vs L2 confusion. Per-axis clamps, per-axis flux limits,\n            and Manhattan-distance bounds all produce \u221a2-anisotropic\n            fields when the physical thing wants L2.\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>Fix the source, not the symptom.</strong>\n            The pinpoint had three rounds of patches: v0.70 (smooth\n            one cell), v0.71 (smooth multi-cell skip region), v0.72\n            (fix the threshold so no cell is skipped). Each was\n            closer to \"don't break it in the first place.\" When a\n            fix is patching the consequences of a guard or\n            threshold, check whether the guard itself is wrong.\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>Test boundary cases of input parameters.</strong>\n            v0.70 worked for the integer-coordinate \"Deluge Now\"\n            button but failed for random clicks. v0.71 worked for\n            most fractional coordinates but failed for offset\n            half-integers. v0.72 needed deliberate testing across\n            integer, half-integer, and offset-fractional epicenters.\n            Test the boundaries of input categories, not just the\n            convenient default.\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>Use conservation laws as diagnostics.</strong>\n            Mass, momentum, divergence, isotropy are all checkable\n            numerically without rendering. Any drift from those\n            invariants over many frames is a signal pointing at a\n            specific subsystem. This is what the hotspot scan tests:\n            \"is the simulation diverging from uniform-mass behavior?\"\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>Tighten one variable at a time.</strong>\n            When something feels off, isolate it: same paint, same\n            splash position, same timestep, vary one parameter,\n            observe. The anisotropy test holds everything but the\n            splash velocity constant; the trace test holds\n            everything but the time axis constant. Multivariate\n            \"this whole thing is broken\" reports almost always\n            resolve to one specific variable's bug.\n          </li>\n        </ol>\n\n        <h2 id=\"maint-uploads\">Source materials and uploads from the original session</h2>\n        <p>\n          The bugs above were diagnosed across many user-uploaded\n          screenshots and screen recordings. The most useful ones\n          (referenced here as a paper trail rather than embedded):\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>Screen recording of the cross during a deluge animation</strong> &mdash; 4-arm wavefront radiating from the splash center. The persistence of this shape across 4 advection modes was the key signal that the issue wasn't in the advection code.</li>\n          <li><strong>Screenshot of a single dark pixel after a deluge cleared the rest of the canvas</strong> &mdash; the pinpoint artifact. Visible against an otherwise clean paper, locating the bug at the splash epicenter rather than in the bulk transport.</li>\n          <li><strong>Multiple screenshots of click-deluge sessions</strong> &mdash; documenting the \"every other click\" and \"every three clicks\" failure patterns that drove v0.71 and v0.72 respectively.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <p>\n          The verification scripts that confirmed each fix are\n          archived in the test harness file. If you find a similar\n          artifact, the patterns above will likely localize it\n          quickly &mdash; or at least point at which subsystem\n          isn't the cause.\n        </p>\n      </section>",
      "old_str": "        <h2 id=\"dig-further-reading\">Further reading</h2>",
      "path": "/home/claude/watercolor.html"
    },
    "message": "Restore Further Reading and add new For Maintainers section after it",

  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-13T02:50:01.621470Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-13T02:50:12.140237Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "tool_use",
    "id": "toolu_015xaBSeNm1ZR94NTEBeu4EJ",
    "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "v0.74 changelog body",
      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.74 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-74\">v0.74 &mdash; Maintainer guide and standalone test harness</h2>\n        <p>\n          No simulation changes. Captures the testing patterns and\n          best practices that emerged across the v0.56-0.72\n          development arc, plus a runnable test harness that\n          implements all of them.\n        </p>\n        <h3>New \"For maintainers\" section in the docs</h3>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>Evidence trail</strong> &mdash; the timeline of user-reported bugs (screen recording of the cross, pinpoint screenshots, \"every other click\" reports), what each one triggered, and how each was diagnosed.</li>\n          <li><strong>Headless test harness</strong> &mdash; how to stub the DOM/Canvas APIs so the lib runs in Node, including the modern-Node fix for read-only <code>navigator</code> and <code>performance</code> globals.</li>\n          <li><strong>Instrumenting the lib</strong> &mdash; the string-replacement pattern that injects <code>_debug_*</code> methods into the lib's public API object without modifying the file. This is how every diagnostic in v0.69-0.72 accessed internal grid arrays.</li>\n          <li><strong>Four diagnostic patterns</strong> &mdash; anisotropy detection (caught the v0.69 cross), per-cell trace (caught the v0.72 pinpoint), hotspot scan (regression check), and CFL bound check (sanity check before changing velocity scales). Each is implemented in the harness and described in prose.</li>\n          <li><strong>Seven best practices</strong> &mdash; lessons distilled from chasing the cross for 13 versions and the pinpoint for 3, in roughly the order I should have applied each.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <h3>Standalone test harness file</h3>\n        <p>\n          <code>washes-test-harness.js</code> ships alongside this\n          HTML page. Single file, ~400 lines, no dependencies.\n          Implements the four diagnostic patterns as runnable CLI\n          subcommands:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">node washes-test-harness.js /path/to/watercolor-lib.js anisotropy\nnode washes-test-harness.js /path/to/watercolor-lib.js trace\nnode washes-test-harness.js /path/to/watercolor-lib.js hotspots\nnode washes-test-harness.js /path/to/watercolor-lib.js cfl\nnode washes-test-harness.js /path/to/watercolor-lib.js all</pre>\n        <p>\n          The harness is designed to be copied and adapted, not\n          imported. Each pattern is a self-contained function with\n          its own injected <code>_debug_*</code> helpers, so you\n          can extract individual patterns and modify them for new\n          diagnostics. The mock DOM is the same shape used in every\n          diagnostic during the v0.56-0.72 arc; the\n          <code>loadLibWithHelpers</code> utility is the same\n          string-replacement trick that exposed internal state\n          without modifying lib source.\n        </p>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.73 ===== -->",
      "old_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.73 ===== -->",
      "path": "/home/claude/watercolor.html"
    },

script_end = None\nfor i, line in enumerate(lines):\n    if line.strip() == '<script>' and script_start is None:\n        script_start = i\n    if script_start is not None and line.strip() == '</script>' and i > script_start:\n        script_end = i; break\nreplacement = \"\"\"  <!-- ================================================================== -->\n  <!--    \u2554\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2557      -->\n  <!--    \u2551   1.  watercolor-lib.js          \u2014 the simulation        \u2551      -->\n  <!--    \u2551   2.  watercolor-v0.74-wiring.js \u2014 the UI glue           \u2551      -->\n  <!--    \u255a\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u255d      -->\n  <!-- ================================================================== -->\n\n  <script>\n// === BEGIN watercolor-lib.js ===\n\"\"\" + lib + \"\"\"\n// === END watercolor-lib.js ===\n  </script>\n\n  <script>\n// === BEGIN watercolor-v0.74-wiring.js ===\n\"\"\" + wiring + \"\"\"\n// === END watercolor-v0.74-wiring.js ===\n  </script>\"\"\"\nnew_lines = lines[:script_start] + [replacement] + lines[script_end+1:]\nopen('/home/claude/v0.74-build.html', 'w').write('\\n'.join(new_lines))\nPY\n\npython3 -c \"\nimport re\nhtml = open('/home/claude/v0.74-build.html').read()\nnc = re.sub(r'<!--.*?-->', '', html, flags=re.DOTALL)\nss = re.findall(r'<script>([\\s\\S]*?)</script>', nc)\nprint(f'{len(ss)} scripts')\nfor i,s in enumerate(ss):\n    open(f'/tmp/v74_s{i+1}.js','w').write(s);     "type": "text",
    "text": "Shipped v0.74 \u2014 the docs HTML plus a standalone test harness. Here's what's new:\n\n## In the docs: a \"For maintainers\" section\n\nSeven subsections covering everything that emerged from the v0.56\u2013v0.72 arc:\n\n**Evidence trail** \u2014 a timeline mapping each user-reported observation (the cross screen recording, the pinpoint screenshots, the \"every other click\" / \"every three clicks\" reports) to the test it triggered and the diagnosis it produced. Six entries spanning v0.61 through v0.72.\n\n**Headless test harness** \u2014 how to stub the DOM/Canvas APIs so the lib runs in Node, including the modern-Node gotcha that `navigator` and `performance` are now read-only getters (use `Object.defineProperty`, not direct assignment).\n\n**Instrumenting the lib** \u2014 the string-replacement trick that injects `_debug_*` methods into the lib's public API object before `eval`. This is how every diagnostic in v0.69\u20130.72 reached internal arrays like `g`, `d`, `u`, `v`, `wet`, `pressure` without modifying the lib file.\n\n**Four diagnostic patterns** with prose + sample output:\n- **Anisotropy** \u2014 caught the v0.69 cross (cardinal/diagonal ratio \u2248 \u221a2)\n- **Per-cell trace** \u2014 caught the v0.72 pinpoint (velocity reversal by frame 10)\n- **Hotspot scan** \u2014 regression check across many fractional epicenters\n- **CFL bound check** \u2014 sanity check before changing velocity scales\n\n**Seven best practices** distilled from the arc, in roughly the order I should have applied each:\n1. Symptoms can be inherited from upstream subsystems (the cross looked like donor-cell but came from the velocity clamp)\n2. Dump numerical state, don't just look at renders\n3. The \u221a2 ratio is a diagnostic tell for L1-vs-L2 confusion\n4. Fix the source, not the symptom\n5. Test boundary cases of input parameters (integer vs half-integer vs offset-fractional)\n6. Use conservation laws as diagnostics\n7. Tighten one variable at a time\n\n**Source materials** \u2014 references to the user-uploaded screenshots and screen recording, what each one showed, and how it shaped the next iteration.\n\n## The test harness file: washes-test-harness.js\n\nSingle file, ~400 lines, no dependencies. Runnable directly:\n\n```bash\nnode washes-test-harness.js /path/to/watercolor-lib.js anisotropy\nnode washes-test-harness.js /path/to/watercolor-lib.js trace\nnode washes-test-harness.js /path/to/watercolor-lib.js hotspots\nnode washes-test-harness.js /path/to/watercolor-lib.js cfl\nnode washes-test-harness.js /path/to/watercolor-lib.js all\n```\n\nI verified all four patterns run cleanly against the current v0.74 lib. The anisotropy ratio is 0.997\u20131.001 (was \u221a2 \u2248 1.42 pre-v0.69), the trace shows neighbor velocities maintaining their outward sign across 30 frames (was reversed by frame 10 pre-v0.72), and the hotspot scan reports only canvas-corner accumulation (was singular epicenter pinpoints pre-v0.72).\n\nEach pattern in the harness is a self-contained function with its own injected `_debug_*` helpers. Extract one, modify it, drop it in as a new diagnostic when the next regression appears.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

      {
        "type": "knowledge",
        "title": "A Guide to Simulating Watercolor Paint with Generative Art | Tyler Hobbs",
        "url": "https://www.tylerxhobbs.com/words/a-guide-to-simulating-watercolor-paint-with-generative-art",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "tylerxhobbs.com",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=tylerxhobbs.com",
          "site_name": "Tyler Hobbs"
        },
        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "... The technique to simulate ... of stacking lots of nearly transparent layers on top of each other. To create the watercolor-like appearance of a gradual fade out, the layers need to have a lot of variation in their shape. However, in areas with sharper boundaries, the layers need less variation. The core of the watercolor technique is a recursive polygon deformation algorithm...\n\nThe algorithm is not particularly exotic. Instead, it's conceptually simple but fine-tuned. I frequently experiment with watercolor paints in my sketchbook. I particularly love the insanely detailed and varied effects that the paints can produce. One sketch caught my eye became the focus while developing this technique. My goal was not to realistically reproduce everything about the watercolor paint. Instead, I aimed to capture the essence of it that I really enjoy. Here\u2019s how it\u2019s done. ... \n\nThe technique to simulate watercolor in generative art consists of stacking lots of nearly transparent layers on top of each other. To create the watercolor-like appearance of a gradual fade out, the layers need to have a lot of variation in their shape. However, in areas with sharper boundaries, the layers need less variation. The core of the watercolor technique is a recursive polygon deformation algorithm.\n\nThe basic watercolor shape is created through one round of polygon deformation. The input polygon is taken and run through the deformation function multiple times (somewhere around 7). The resulting polygon becomes the \"base polygon\" for all of the layers we will create. ... For each layer, start with the \"base polygon\" and run it through the deformation function several more times (maybe 4 or 5). This will produce a polygon that is similar to the base polygon, but differs in all of the fine details.         "text": "Let\u2019s delve into some core techniques, such as using the recursive polygon deformation algorithm and applying texture masking to create stunning digital watercol\n\nOk, let\u2019s talk about making your digital art look like watercolors with a cool trick. This trick is called the recursive polygon deformation algorithm. Here\u2019s how it works: you start with a shape made of straight lines, like a square or triangle. For every line in that shape, you find the middle point and move that point a little bit to make a new spot using a special math rule (Gaussian distribution). Then replace the old line with two new lines that go through this new spot. Keep doing this over and over, about seven times. This process makes the edges of your shapes wiggly, kind of like how watercolor paint spreads on paper. It\u2019s perfect for giving your digital paintings that soft, fluid look of real watercolors. Imagine drawing a tree; instead of rigid lines for branches, they now have natural curves and waves.",

        "title": "2023Interactive Watercolor Painting | Cross Reality Collaboration Sandbox Research Group",
        "url": "https://sites.uw.edu/crcs/projects/2023interactive-watercolor-painting/",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "uw.edu",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=uw.edu",

        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "The physically-based approach presents watercolor patterns by emulating the physical dynamics of paint and water flow and rendering an image based on the simulated results. The Lattice-Boltzmann method (LBM) is favored by researchers in watercolor fluid dynamic simulation due to its computation ...\n\nTo offer more freedom to watercolor creation, simulation of watercolor painting has been extensively studied including physically-based methods. The physically-based approach presents watercolor patterns by emulating the physical dynamics of paint and water flow and rendering an image based on the simulated results. The Lattice-Boltzmann method (LBM) is favored by researchers in watercolor fluid dynamic simulation due to its computation efficiency and stability in dealing with complex boundaries, incorporation of microscopic interactions, and potentials for parallel implementation. \n\nThe project follows Chu and Tai\u2019s approach of modeling hydrodynamics with LBM and using the Kubelka-Munk (KM) reflectance model rendering method proposed by Curtis et al. Compared to other methods, this approach strikes a balance between accuracy of the simulation model and execution time.\n\nThe LBE models fluid flow as a continuous propagation and collision process on a discrete lattice where the multiple lattices can be processed in parallel. By trading-off realism, LBE is capable of presenting relatively realistic watercolor results, while the parallel processing significantly reduces the simulation time. The implementation of the physically-based simulation requires a platform that supports parallel processing at the per-pixel level, user\u2019s interactive drawing activities, and rendering of the simulation results. \n\nUnity3D, as a cross-platform game engine, is chosen for the implementation of the system because of its support for user-defined HLSH shaders that can process pixel operations in parallel, a well-designed editor that can accommodate complex parameter adjustments, and pre-bui",
        "is_citable": true,

          "url": "https://sites.uw.edu/crcs/projects/2023interactive-watercolor-painting/",

        }

        "text": "Additionally, executable files without an API do not integrate seamlessly with widely used analytical workflows.\n\nThis paper introduces *dlmoR*, an open-source R package implementing the hockey-stick method.         "url": "https://arxiv.org/pdf/1910.06932",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "arxiv.org",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=arxiv.org",
          "site_name": "arxiv"
        },
        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "We conduct a largescale study of publication citations with 319,438,977 comments collected from 25,925 active repositories written in seven programming languages. Our findings indicate that academic publications can be knowledge sources for software development. These referenced publications are particularly from journals. In terms of knowledge transfer, algorithm is the most prevalent type of knowledge transferred from the publications, with proposed formulas or equations typically implemented in methods or functions in source code files. In a closer look at GitHub repositories referencing academic publications, we find that science-related repositories are the most frequent among GitHub repositories with publication citations, and that the vast majority of these publications are referenced by repository owners who are different from the publication\n\nAkira Inokuchi, Yusuf Sulistyo Nugroho, Supatsara Wattanakriengkrai, Fumiaki Konishi, Hideaki Hata, Kenichi Matsumoto",

        "url": "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2305.15690",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",

          "site_name": "arxiv"

        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "They were evaluated using problem-solving competition datasets such as CodeSearchNet and Google Code Jam and have not shown to be useful for finding algorithm implementations in software repositories.\n\nOur work aims to search open-source projects to find algorithm implementations. We use pseudo code as a search query. Pseudo code is available in textbooks and papers, typically proofread. It provides important computation steps compared to a short natural language query; thus, more details are available for accurate search. Pseudo code is system-independent and can be applied to search for different programming languages. Our search results are the code fragments that implement the algorithms located within a single method or across multiple methods and thus are more fine-grained than returning a method, as most existing search tools do.\n\nOur approach consists of three components: algorithm analysis, representation learning, and vector-based code retrieval.",

        "text": "# GitHub Repositories with Links to Academic Papers: Open Access, Traceability, and Evolution\n\nSupatsara Wattanakriengkrai<sup>a</sup>, Bodin Chinthanet<sup>a</sup>, Hideaki Hata<sup>a</sup>, Raula Gaikovina Kula<sup>a</sup>, Christoph Treude<sup>b</sup>, Jin Guo<sup>c</sup>, Kenichi Matsumoto<sup>a</sup>\n\n<sup>a</sup> Nara Institute of Science and Technology, Japan\n<sup>b</sup> University of Adelaide, Australia\n<sup>c</sup> McGill University, Canada\n\n#### Abstract\n\nTraceability between published scientific breakthroughs and their implementation is essential, especially in the case of Open Source Software implements bleeding edge science into its code.         "title": "Using the Full-text Content of Academic Articles to Identify and   Evaluate Algorithm Entities in the Domain of Natural Language Processing",
        "url": "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2010.10817",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",

          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=arxiv.org",

        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "# Using the Full-text Content of Academic Articles to Identify and Evaluate Algorithm Entities in the Domain of Natural Language Processing\n\nYuzhuo Wang, Chengzhi Zhang\\*\n\nDepartment of Information Management, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, 210094, China\n\nAbstract: In the era of big data, the advancement, improvement, and application of algorithms in academic research have played an important role in promoting the development of different disciplines. Academic papers in various disciplines, especially computer science, contain a large number of algorithms. Identifying the algorithms from the full-text content of papers can determine popular or classical algorithms in a specific field and help scholars gain a comprehensive understanding of the algorithms and even the field. To this end, this article takes the field of natural language processing (NLP) as an example and identifies algorithms from academic papers in the field. \n\nA dictionary of algorithms is constructed by manually annotating the contents of papers, and sentences containing algorithms in the dictionary are extracted through dictionary-based matching. The number of articles mentioning an algorithm is used as an indicator to analyze the influence of that algorithm. Our results reveal the algorithm with the highest influence in NLP papers and show that classification algorithms represent the largest proportion among the high-impact algorithms. In addition, the evolution of the influence of algorithms reflects the changes in research tasks and topics in the field, and the changes in the influence of different algorithms show different trends. As a preliminary exploration, this paper conducts an analysis of the impact of algorithms mentioned in the academic text, and the results can be used as training data for the automatic extraction of large-scale algorithms in the future. \n\nFor many researchers, especially beginners in a field, gaining a thorough understanding of the algorithms a",

        "title": "Computer generated watercolor",
        "url": "https://image-ppubs.uspto.gov/dirsearch-public/print/downloadPdf/6198489",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",

          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=uspto.gov",
          "site_name": "uspto"
        },
        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "What is claimed is: 1. \n\nA method for producing a watercolor simulation that represents at least one wash applied to paper, each wash including at least one pigment dispersed in a fluid, comprising the steps of: (a) providing a fluid model that includes a plurality of layers for each wash included in the watercolor simulation, wherein said plurality of layers comprise: (i) a shallow fluid layer in which the wash flows above a surface of the paper; (ii) a pigment deposition layer where a pigment is adsorbed and desorbed by the paper; and (iii) a capillary layer within the paper in which the wash is diffused by a capillary action; (b) as a function of a plurality of predefined parameters and in accord with the fluid model, specifying how each wash flows over the paper to determine quantities of each pigment (c) applying an optical composite model to render the watercolor simulation as a function of optical characteristics of each pigment and as a function of the quantities of each pigment deposited at the plurality of discrete spatial locations on the paper. 2. \n\nThe method of claim 1, wherein the plurality of parameters include at least one of: (a) physical properties of each pigment employed in producing the watercolor simulation; (b) physical properties of the paper used for the watercolor simulation; and (c) predetermined values used in defining the fluid model. 3. The method of claim 1, wherein the quantities of each pigment deposited at the plurality of discrete spatial locations are stored in an array, and wherein a data structure that includes the arrays for the pigments used in a wash comprises a glaze. 4. The method of claim 1, wherein a Kubelka-Munk model is employed as the optical composite model for rendering the watercolor simulation. 5. The method of claim 1, wherein the step of specifying how each wash flows over the paper includes the step of determining a movement of the wash in the shallow fluid layer, as a function of a velocity of the wash as it m",
        "is_citable": true,

        "type": "knowledge",
        "title": "Watercolor simulation in computer graphics",
        "url": "https://image-ppubs.uspto.gov/dirsearch-public/print/downloadPdf/5432896",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "uspto.gov",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=uspto.gov",
          "site_name": "uspto"
        },
        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "SUMMARY OF THE INVENTION The present invention makes it possible for a watercolor artist to electronically paint on a computer screen in the same manner and with the same techniques as he would use on paper. The invention gives the artist the \"feel\" of drawing on wet paper with familiar watercolor media by combining three features: a) the ability to premix colors on a palette; b) the ability to reproduce the behavior of various brush materials and brush textures as the brush runs out of paint toward the end of a brush stroke; and c) the ability to simulate the smearing effect of a brush being drawn over a still wet previously drawn color on paper with water content. All of these abilities are achieved by the continual recalculation of all screen pixels lying within the brush pattern at any given time during the brush stroke, in accordance with algorithms more specifically described in this specification.",

        "type": "knowledge",
        "title": "How to Find a US Patent's Expiration Date - Patent Sunset",
        "url": "https://patentsunset.com/blog/how-to-find-a-patent-expiration-date",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "patentsunset.com",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=patentsunset.com",
          "site_name": "Patent Sunset"
        },
        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "Ignoring continuation chains. A continuation patent often has an effective filing date years before it was filed. The expiration date is calculated from that earlier date. Not checking maintenance fees. A patent that looks valid based on dates alone may have already expired for nonpayment.\n\nPTE and PTA cannot both add to the term simultaneously in most cases and there are caps on the total extended term. A terminal disclaimer is a filing that cuts a patent's life short. Patent owners sometimes file them voluntarily to overcome an obviousness rejection when they already own a related patent. The disclaimer says the patent won't outlast an earlier patent. Terminal disclaimers appear in the prosecution history on Patent Center. They can shorten a patent's actual expiration date significantly, sometimes by years. Utility patents require maintenance fee payments at 3.5, 7.5 and 11.5 years after grant. If the fees go unpaid, the patent expires early. \n\nThe patent may be reinstated if fees are paid within a grace period, but if that window closes, the patent is dead. The USPTO's Patent Center and the Patent Application Information Retrieval (PAIR) system both show maintenance fee status.\n\nThe expiration clock for a utility patent starts at the filing date, not the grant date. The standard term is 20 years from the earliest effective filing date. For patents filed before June 8, 1995, older rules apply (more on that in a separate post). Design patents are different. They expire 15 years from grant, not 20 years from filing. Plant patents also have their own rules. For a utility patent filed after June 7, 1995, identify the earliest effective filing date. If it's a continuation or divisional, that date might be years before the patent was granted. If the USPTO took longer than it was supposed to during examination, the patent owner may be entitled to extra days tacked onto the back end. This is called patent term adjustment. \n\nPTA is calculated by the USPTO and listed on the",

        }

        "title": "How do I figure out if a US patent is still in force? - Brown & Michaels",
        "url": "https://bpmlegal.com/content/howtoterm",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "bpmlegal.com",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=bpmlegal.com",
          "site_name": "Bpmlegal"
        },
        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "Utility Patents and Plant Patents based on applications which were pending on June 8, 1995 (actual filing date, not priority date), and any Utility or Plant Patent which was issued on or after June 7, 1978 and had not expired before June 8, 1995, have a term measured by the longer of 17 years from the date of issue or 20 years from the date of US filing* of the earliest non-provisional** application in its chain of parentage. *= By \"US filing date\" we mean, if the US application claims priority from an earlier-filed foreign application (that is, it gets the benefit of a filing date in a foreign patent office for purposes of determining what is \"prior art\"), it is the actual filing date of the first application in the USPTO which starts the 20-year term, not the filing date of the foreign application.",
        "is_citable": true,

      {
        "type": "knowledge",
        "title": "Bacon & Thomas Learning Center: Patent Term Calculation",
        "url": "https://www.baconthomas.com/p_terms.html",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "baconthomas.com",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=baconthomas.com",
          "site_name": "Baconthomas"

        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "For applications filed on or after June 8, 1995, the patent term is 20 years from the filing date of the earliest U.S. application to which priority is claimed (excluding provisional applications). For applications that were pending on and for patents that were still in force on June 8, 1995, ...\n\nThe patent term may also be reduced by any disclaimer (called a \"terminal disclaimer\") to the patent term. Reexamined Patent A reexamined patent expires on the day the original granted patent would have ordinarily expired. Terminal Disclaimer If a patent application is obvious in light of one of the applicant's existing patents, the applicant may be required by the USPTO to disclaim a part of the term. For example, an applicant's patent A expires on December 24, 2000. The applicant filed another patent application two years later. Under some conditions, the second patent might expire later than the first. \n\nIf the applicant files a terminal disclaimer, then the second patent will expire at the same time as the first, the extra portion having been disclaimed. In filing the terminal disclaimer, the applicant also agrees that the second patent will only be enforceable if it and the first patent are commonly owned.\n\nFor applications filed on or after June 8, 1995, the patent term is 20 years from the filing date of the earliest U.S. application to which priority is claimed (excluding provisional applications). For applications that were pending on and for patents that were still in force on June 8, 1995, the patent term is either 17 years from the issue date or 20 years from the filing date of the earliest U.S. application to which priority is claimed (excluding provisional applications), the longer term applying. Design Patent Design patents, unlike utility patents, have a term of 14 years from the date of issue.",

        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "# ACCESSING UNITED STATES BULK PATENT DATA WITH patentpy AND patentr\n\n#### A PREPRINT\n\nJames Yu, BS School of Medicine Case Western Reserve University \u2022 Hayley Beltz, MS\nDepartment of Astronomy\nUniversity of Michigan\n\nMilind Y. Desai, MD, MBA\nDepartment of Cardiovascular Medicine\nHeart, Vascular & Thoracic Institute\nCleveland Clinic\n\nP\u00e9ter \u00c9rdi, PhD, DSc Department of Physics Center for Complex Systems Studies Kalamazoo College Jacob G. Scott, MD, DPhil Translational Hematology & Oncology Research Lerner Research Institute Cleveland Clinic\n\n\u00ae Raoul R. Wadhwa, BA\nCleveland Clinic Lerner College of Medicine\nCase Western Reserve University\n\nJuly 20, 2021\n\n#### **ABSTRACT**\n\nThe United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) provides publicly accessible bulk data files containing information for all patents from 1976 onward. However, the format of these files changes over time and is memory-inefficient, which can pose issues for individual researchers. \n\nAs a result of these challenges, potential users face a high barrier to entry prior to taking advantage of the available data. The *patentpy* and *patentr* packages simplify accessing USPTO data by providing programmatic interfaces that return it in a single rectangular, tidy [1] format and significantly reduce its storage size.\n\nThe *patentpy* package provides a Python interface with the functionality implemented using a combination of Python and C++. The *patentr* package does the same with an R interface. Since the two packages share functionality, they also share a C++ code base, with each depending on existing XML libraries in the respective language. Of note, Python and R both boast portability across multiple operating systems, making the corresponding packages easily available to a large number of users.\n\nCurrently, the most authoritative reference for patent analytics is the World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO) Manual. \n\nWe process the data by extracting the year from each patent's issue date",

        "title": "How to Tell if a Patent Is Expired: 2025 Expert Guide",
        "url": "https://thompsonpatentlaw.com/how-to-tell-if-a-patent-is-expired/",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",

          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=thompsonpatentlaw.com",

        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "For example, it might show \u201cFee Payment \u2013 Year 4 \u2013 Paid on [date]\u201d. If a fee was missed, the record indicates that the patent has a status, such as \u201cLapsed for failure to pay maintenance fees,\u201d as of a specific date.\n\nPatent owners can pay the maintenance fee up to 6 months before the due date, or pay late within a grace period: On-time payment: anytime from 6 months before the due date through the exact due date. Grace period: up to 6 months after the due date, with a surcharge. If the fee isn\u2019t paid by the end of that 6-month grace period, the patent expires. There\u2019s technically another 6-month window for possible revival with a heavier surcharge if the lapse was unintentional, but after that, expiration is usually final. ... You can see maintenance fee records via the USPTO\u2019s Fees lookup or in Patent Center. It lists each fee due date and indicates whether it has been paid. For example, it might show \u201cFee Payment \u2013 Year 4 \u2013 Paid on [date]\u201d. \n\nIf a fee was missed, the record indicates that the patent has a status, such as \u201cLapsed for failure to pay maintenance fees,\u201d as of a specific date.\n\nThe average PTA for patents issued since 2005 is approximately 1 year (median around 290 days) for those that receive an adjustment. Over 25% of patents receive more than a year of added time, and a small percentage receive multi-year extensions due to significant delays in the patent process. If a patent\u2019s 20-year term would end January 1, 2025, but it has 411 days of PTA, its actual expiration will be February 6, 2026. This means the basic term is extended past the original expiration date due to these adjustments. In some cases, the PTA exceeded 4 years, often due to lengthy appeals or interference proceedings. One study in 2024 found that approximately 5% of patents received over 1,000 days (approximately 3 years) of extra term, and 1% received over 4 years of extra term. \n\nThese tend to be in fields such as software, where the USPTO has hist",

        "title": "Large-Scale Historical Watermark Recognition: dataset and a new   consistency-based approach",
        "url": "https://arxiv.org/pdf/1908.10254",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "arxiv.org",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=arxiv.org",
          "site_name": "arxiv"
        },
        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "# Large-Scale Historical Watermark Recognition: dataset and a new consistency-based approach\n\nXi Shen, Ilaria Pastrolin, Oumayma Bounou, Spyros Gidaris, Marc Smith, Olivier Poncet, Mathieu Aubry\n\n**Abstract**\u2014Historical watermark recognition is a highly practical, yet unsolved challenge for archivists and historians. With a large number of well-defined classes, cluttered and noisy samples, different types of representations, both subtle differences between classes and high intra-class variation, historical watermarks are also challenging for pattern recognition. In this paper, overcoming the difficulty of data collection, we present a large public dataset with more than 6k new photographs, allowing for the first time to tackle at scale the scenarios of practical interest for scholars: one-shot instance recognition and cross-domain one-shot instance recognition amongst more than 16k fine-grained classes. \n\nThis allowed us to collect more than 6k unique samples for hundreds of classes. Moreover, we searched and photographed systematically the exact original watermarks corresponding to hundreds of line drawings printed in Briquet's classic catalog of approximately 17k watermarks [5], creating a cross-domain database, which allows to directly evaluate a task of high practical interest: cross-domain one-shot fine-grained recognition. Our dataset is the first watermark dataset with such characteristics, allowing for the first time to train and evaluate deep learning algorithms for fine-grained and cross-domain watermark recognition. We demonstrate results that are of high enough quality to be of immediate practical interest, recognizing a watermark from a single photograph amongst more than 16k fine-grained classes described by a single drawing with 55% top-1 accuracy.\n\nThe rest of the paper is organized as follows. First we discuss related work in Section 2.",

        "text": "---\ncanonical: https://patents.google.com/patent/US6198489B1/en\nmeta-DC.contributor: University of Washington\nmeta-DC.date: 2001-03-06\nmeta-DC.description:\n     A watercolor model based on an ordered set of translucent glazes, which are created independently usinig a shallow water fluid simulation. A Kubelka-Munk compositing model is used for simulating the optical effect of the superimposed glazes. The computer generated watercolor model is used as part of an interactive watercolor paint system, or as a method for automatic image \u201cwatercolorization.\u201d\n  \n  \nmeta-DC.relation: US:5784301\nmeta-DC.title: Computer generated watercolor\n  \nmeta-DC.type: patent\nmeta-citation_patent_application_number: US:09/025,412\nmeta-citation_patent_number: US:6198489\nmeta-citation_pdf_url: https://patentimages.storage.googleapis.com/2e/27/c8/424860f98d9142/US6198489.pdf\nmeta-citation_reference: Strassmann, Steve, &quot;Hairy Brushes,&quot; SIGGRAPH '86, Dallas, Aug. 18-22, vol. 20, No. 4, 1986, pp. 225-232.\nmeta-description:\n     A watercolor model based on an ordered set of translucent glazes, which are created independently usinig a shallow water fluid simulation. A Kubelka-Munk compositing model is used for simulating the optical effect of the superimposed glazes. The computer generated watercolor model is used as part of an interactive watercolor paint system, or as a method for automatic image \u201cwatercolorization.\u201d\n  \n  \nmeta-referrer: origin-when-crossorigin\nmeta-viewport: width=device-width, initial-scale=1\ntitle: US6198489B1 - Computer generated watercolor\n        - Google Patents\n---\n\n\n# US6198489B1 - Computer generated watercolor - Google Patents\n\nComputer generated watercolor [Download PDF](https://patentimages.storage.googleapis.com/2e/27/c8/424860f98d9142/US6198489.pdf)\n\n## Info\n\nPublication number\nUS6198489B1\n\nUS6198489B1 US09/025,412 US2541298A US6198489B1 US 6198489 B1 US6198489 B1 US 6198489B1     US 2541298 A US2541298 A US 2541298A US 6198489 B1 US6198489 B1 US 6198489B1Authority\nUS\n\nUnited States\n\nPrior art keywords\npigment\n\npaper\n\nwash\n\nwatercolor\n\nsimulation\n\nPrior art date\n1997-02-21\n\nLegal status (The legal status is an assumption and is not a legal conclusion.
