US06198489-20010306-M00001](https://patentimages.storage.googleapis.com/e6/8e/a9/59e382a0ad8318/US06198489-20010306-M00001.png)](https://patentimages.storage.googleapis.com/e6/8e/a9/59e382a0ad8318/US06198489-20010306-M00001.png)\n\nThese equations are spatially discretized on a staggered grid. The staggered grid representation stores velocity values at grid cell boundaries and other values (pressure, pigment, etc.) at grid cell centers. The standard notation for staggered grids is used: integer indices (like the \u201ci\u201d and \u201cj\u201d in pi, j) are grid cell centers, and indices incremented or decreinented by \u00bd (like \u201ci+\u00bd\u201d in ui+\u00bdj) j) are used for grid cell boundaries. The equations are discretized in time and solved forward using Euler's Method, with an adaptive step size. The step size is reduced if velocities exceed one pixel per time step.\n\nA gravity term is added to account for the local slope paperslope of the surface of the paper. This term is added to the velocities before the fluid step is computed.\n\nThe final pseudo code (with reference to the corresponding blocks in FIG. 7) for integrating the fluid simulation forward one time unit is as follows:\n\nproc MoveFluid(u, v, p, k, u, \u0394t, paperslope)\n\n/* Subtract paper slope from velocities u and v, as noted in a block **68** */\n\nu\u2212=paperslopex\n\nv\u2212=paperslopey\n\n/* Determine if either velocity is greater than zero, as provided in a decision block **70** */\n\nif (max(u)>1 or max(v)>1)\n\n/* If so, then reduce the step size, in accord with a block **72** */\n\nthen reduce stepsize \u0394t\n\n/* Otherwise, set time to zero, as noted in a block **74**, get the first cell, as noted in a block **76**, and begin a loop */\n\nfor t from 0 to 1 by \u0394t do\n\nforall cells (i,j) do\n\n/* Update the velocity in current cell using Euler integration, as indicated in a block **78** */\n\n *\u00fb* i+\u00bd,j *=u* i+\u00bd,j+\u0394t(\u00bc(*u* i\u2212\u00bd,j *+u* i+\u00bd,j)2\u2212\u00bc(*u* i+\u00bd,j *+u* i+{fraction (3/2)},j)2\n\n+\u00bc(*u* i+\u00bd,j\u22121 *+u* i+\u00bd,j)(*v* i,j\u2212\u00bd *+v* i\u22121,j+\u00bd)\n\n\u2212\u00bc(*u* i\u2212\u00bd,j *+u* i+\u00bd,j+1)(*v* i,j+\u00bd *+v* i+1,j+\u00bd)\n\n+*u*(*u* i+{fraction (3/2)},j *+u* i\u2212\u00bd,j *+u* i+\u00bd,j+1 *+u* i+\u00bd,j\u22121\u22124*u* i+\u00bd,j)\n\n+(*p* i,j *\u2212p* i+1,j)\u2212ke\u22120.1(pi,j+pi+1,j)/2 *u* i+\u00bd,j)\n\n{circumflex over (v)}i,j+\u00bd *=v* i,j+\u00bd+\u0394t(\u00bc(*v* i,j\u2212\u00bd *+v* i,j+\u00bd)2\u2212\u00bc(*v* i,j+\u00bd *+u* i,j+{fraction (3/2)})2\n\n+\u00bc(*u* i\u2212\u00bd,j *+u* i\u2212\u00bd,j+1)(*v* i\u22121,j+\u00bd *+v* i,j+\u00bd)\n\n\u2212\u00bc(*u* i+\u00bd,j *+u* i+\u00bd,j+1)(*v* i,j+\u00bd *+v* i+1,j+\u00bd)\n\n+*u*(*v* i+1,j+\u00bd *+v* i\u22121,j+\u00bd *+v* i,j+{fraction (3/2)} *+v* i,j\u2212\u00bd\u22124*v* i,j+\u00bd)\n\n+(*p* i,j *+p* i,j+1)\u2212ke\u22120.1(pi,j+pi,j+1)/2 *v* i,j+\u00bd)\n\n/* Determine if there are any more cells, as noted in a decision block **80**, and if so, get the next cell, as provided in a block **82**. Once all cells have been processed, add a step to time, as provided in a block **84** */\n\nendfor\n\n/* Determine if current time is greater than or equal to one, in a decision block **86**, and if so, stop; otherwise, loop back block **76** in an outer FOR loop, to repeat the step in block **78** for each cell /*\n\nendfor\n\nu=\u00fb\n\nv={circumflex over (v)}\n\nendproc\n\nReducing Divergence\n\nThe present invention provides for reducing the divergence of the velocity field (\u2202u/\u2202v+\u2202v/\u2202y) after each time step until it is less than maxdiv by redistributing the fluid into neighboring grid cells. This step does not have as clear a physical basis in the two-dimensional (2D) shallow water simulation as it has in a related prior art three-dimensional (3D) simulation. However, the step appears to make a significant difference for reducing ringing artifacts in the simulation, and has thus been found to be useful. In this approach, relax, maxdiv and maxsteps are parameters that control aspects of the simulation (see the table in FIG. 3 for the symbols/values used in a current preferred embodiment, their definitions, and an indication of the procedure in which these symbols/values are used). The steps implemented in reducing divergence are shown in FIG. **8** and the blocks describing these steps and corresponding pseudo code are as follows:\n\nproc ReduceDivergence(u, v, p, relax, maxdiv, maxsteps)\n\nlocal dp, div, count, maxdp;\n\n/* A block **90** sets a count to zero to initialize the process. The divergence is determined, as noted in a block **92**, and a decision block **94** provides for determining whether the divergence is less than or equal to the maximum divergence, maxdiv, and if so, the procedure stops. If not, a decision block **96** determines if the count is greater than a maximum count, maxsteps, and if so, the procedure stops */\n\nwhile ((div>maxdiv) and (count<1maxsteps)) do\n\n/* If the response to both decision blocks **94** and **96** is negative, a block **98** provides for getting a first cell, as the initial step in an inner loop */\n\nforall cells (i,j) do\n\n/* Fluid pressure is dispersed locally around current cell, as noted in a block **100** */\n\ndp=-relax*(ui+\u00bd,j\u2212ui+\u00bd,j *+v* i+\u00bd,j\u2212vi+\u00bd,j\u2212)\n\npi,j+=dp /* Note: p modified in place */\n\n\u00fbi+\u00bd,j+=dp\n\n\u00fbi\u2212\u00bd,j\u2212=dp\n\n{circumflex over (v)}i,j+\u00bd+=dp\n\n{circumflex over (v)}i,j\u2212\u00bd\u2212=dp\n\nmaxdp=max(dp, maxdp)\n\n/* A decision block **102** determines if there are any more cells to be processed, and if so, a block **104** provided for getting the next cell for input to the process in block **100** */\n\nendfor\n\n/* If there are not any more cells to be processed in decision block **102**, a block **106** provides for incrementing the count, and then the process loops back to block **92** */\n\ncount+=1\n\nendwhile\n\nu=\u00fb\n\nv={circumflex over (v)}\n\nreturn maxdiv\n\nendproc\n\nMoving Pigments in the Shallow Water Layer\n\nPigments move within the top layer as specified by the velocity field u, \u03bdcomputed for the water set forth above. In this part of the simulation, the relative quantity of pigment in each cell is reduced according to the rate of fluid movement out of the cell. This pigment is then distributed to the neighboring cells appropriately, as defined by the steps indicated in the blocks of FIG. **9** and by the following pseudo code, which corresponds to the blocks as indicated:\n\nproc MovePigment(gs[1. . . upigs], u, v, \u0394t)\n\n/* A block **110** provides for setting the variable NSTEPS to the reciprocal of the time step */\n\nnsteps=1/\u0394t\n\n/* A block **112** provides for getting the first pigment included in the pigments used, a block **114** sets the step to zero, and a block **116** provides for getting the first cell */\n\nforall p\u03b5npigs do\n\nfor nsteps do\n\n{circumflex over (gs)}=gs\n\nforall cells (i,j) do\n\n/* A block **118** provides for dispersing the current pigment locally around the current cell; a decision block **120** then determines if there are any more cells, and if so, a block **122** provides for getting the next cell, so that the dispersion process implemented in block **118** can be repeated for the next cell. If not, a block **124** indicates that the step variable is incremented before proceeding with a decision block **126** */\n\n{circumflex over (gs)}[p]i+1,j\u2212=max(ui+\u00bd,j gs[p]i,j 0)\n\n{circumflex over (gs)}[p]i\u22121,j\u2212=max(\u2212ui\u2212\u00bd,j gs[p]i,j 0)\n\n{circumflex over (gs)}[p]i,j+1\u2212=max(vi,j+\u00bd gs[p]i,j 0)\n\n{circumflex over (gs)}[p]i,j\u22121\u2212=max(vi,j\u2212\u00bd gs[p]i,j 0)\n\n{circumflex over (gs)}[p]i,j\u2212=max(ui+\u00bd,jgs[p]i,j 0)+max(\u2212ui\u2212\u00bd,j gs[p]i,j,0)+max(*v* i,j+\u00bd gs[p]i,j,0)+max(*v* i,j\u2212\u00bdgs[p]i,j,0)\n\n/* Decision block **126** determines if the current value of the step variable is less than that of the variable nsteps, and if so, the logic loops back to block **116**. Otherwise, the logic proceeds to a decision block **128**, which determines if there are any more pigments to be processed, and if so, a block **130** provides for getting the next pigment, before looping back to block **114** */\n\nendfor\n\ngs={circumflex over (gs)}\n\nendfor\n\nendfor\n\n/* A negative response to decision block **128**, terminates the process */ p={circumflex over (p)}\n\nendproc\n\nPigment Adsorption and Desorption\n\nAt each step of the simulation, pigment is also adsorbed by the pigment deposition layer at a certain rate, and desorbed back into the fluid at another rate. Note that any initial pigment placed outside of any wet-area mask **28** is immediately entirely adsorbed by the pigment deposition layer. The parameters deposit[p] and lift[p], which are included in the table of FIG. 3, are scalars representing the rate of adsorption and desorption. These parameters determine how quickly pigment is deposited onto the paper. The exposure[p] parameter determines how much the paper height b affects lift and deposit rates, and its value can be selected so as to imitate pigments that are heavier and tend to sediment out into the valleys of the paper. FIG. 10 shows the logical steps implemented in this adsorption/desorption process, and these steps are reflected in the following pseudo code, which defines the AdsorbPigments procedure:\n\nproc AdsorbPigment(gs[1. . . npigs], gd[1. . . npigs], b, deposit[1. . . npigs], lift[1. . . npigs],\n\nexposure[1. . . npigs])\n\n/* A block **132** in the Figure provides for getting the first pigment, while a block **134** provides for getting the first cell */ local Ddown, Dup\n\nforall p\u03b5npigs do\n\nforall cells(i,j) do\n\n/* As provided in a block **136**, the pigment in the current cell is desorbed, while a block **138** notes that the pigment in the current cell is absorbed */\n\nDdown=gd[p] lift[p] (1+exposure[p](b\u22121))\n\nDup=gs[p] deposit[p] (1\u2212exposure[p]b)\n\nif ((gd[p]+Ddown)>1)\n\nthen Ddowin=max(1\u2212gs[p], 0)\n\nif ((gs[p]+Dup)>1)\n\nthen Dup=max(1\u2212gd[p], 0)\n\ngd[p]+=Ddown\u2212Dup\n\ngs[p]+=Dup\u2212Ddown\n\n/* A decision block **140** determines if there are any more cells, and if so, a block **142** provides for getting the next cell prior to repeating the desorption and adsorption process of  block  **136** and **138**. If there are no more cells, the logic advances to a decision block **144** */\n\nendfor\n\n/* Decision block **144** determines if there are any more pigments, and if so, advances to a block **146**, which provides for getting the next pigment */\n\nendfor\n\n/* A negative response to decision block **144** terminates the process */\n\nendproc\n\nEdge Darkening\n\nIt is conjectured that edge darkening arises from increased evaporation near the edges of a small puddle, due to the relatively larger surface area of fluid per unit area of paper near the edge. The following routine, which corresponds to the logic shown in FIG. 11, simulates this effect, i.e., removing water from the edges. The water removal causes a slight fluid flow toward the edges and in turn brings in more pigment. The procedure and corresponding blocks are as follows:\n\nproc DarkenEdges(p, mask, edgeKer, darkening)\n\n/* A block **150** provides for getting the first cell */\n\nblur=a Gaussian blurred version of mask with kernel edgeKer\n\n/* A decision block **152** determines if the mask at the current position is>zero, i.e., if the cell is within the mask area */\n\nforall cells (i,j) do\n\nif (maski,j>0)\n\n/* If the response to decision block **152** is affirmative, a block **154** calls for reducing fluid pressure in the current cell in proportion to the Gaussian blur of the mask at the current cell position */\n\nthen pi,j\u2212=max(min(blurij* darkening, pi,j), 0)\n\n/* The logic proceeds to a decision block **156** if the response to decision block **152** is negative or after the step in block **154**. Decision block **156** determines if there are any more cells, and if so, a block **158** provides for getting the next cell before looping back to decision block **152** */\n\nendfor\n\n/* A negative response to decision block **156** results in termination of the process */\n\nendproc\n\nDiffusing Water through the Capillary Layer\n\nThe following routine is used in the present invention to simulate the diffusion of water through the bottom, capillary layer, according to the paper's holding capacity pcap, which specifies the volume not occupied by paper fibers. This routine can also expand wet-area mask **28**, which creates synthetic \u201cbackrun\u201d effects. The routine takes as its parameters: \u03b5, the minimum saturation a pixel must have before it can diffuse to its neighbors; \u03b4, a value below which a pixel will not receive diffusion; and \u03c3, the minimum saturation required to expand the wet-area to include a pixel. The logic implemented in the routine is illustrated in FIG. 12, with reference to the following pseudo code:\n\nproc DiffuseWater(mask, pcap, q, absorption, \u03b5, \u03b4, \u03c3)\n\n/* A block **160** provides for getting the first cell */\n\nforall cells (i,j) do\n\n/* A decision block **162** determines if the mask at the current cell\n\nposition is greater than zero */\n\nif (maski,j>0) then\n\n/* An affirmative response to decision block **162** leads to a block **164**, which provides for increasing the water quantity in the current cell as its capacity allows\n\n/* qi,j+=max(min(absorption, pcapi,j\u2212qi,j), 0) */\n\nendfor\n\n/* Following a negative response to decision block **162** and after block **164**, the logic proceeds to a decision block **166**, to determine if there are any more cells, and if so, proceeds to a block **168**, to get the next cell. A negative response to decision block **166** leads to a block **170** in which the water is diffused into the cells. A diffusion constant of about 0.25 is employed in the preferred embodiment, but flow is limited to the capacities of the neighboring cells. Also, there is no diffusion between two cells unless the sending cell has more than \u03b5 water, and the receiving cell has less than \u03b4. The wet-area mask is shrink/expand. Add cells with water level above a to the wet-area mask, as indicated in a block **172** */\n\nendproc\n\nPaper Generation\n\nPaper texture is modeled as a height field and a fluid capacity field. The height field is generated using one of a selection of pseudo-random processes. The fluid capacity per pixel is computed from the height field, cap=ht*(capmax\u2212capmin)+capmin. Some examples of synthetic paper textures on a simulated watercolor are shown in Plates **6**A-**6**E.\n\nRendering the Pigmented Layers\n\nThe KM model is used to perform the optical compositing of glazing layers in the preferred embodiment of the present invention. In the present use of the KM model, each pigment is assigned a set of absorption coefficients K and scattering coefficients S. These coefficients are a function of wavelength, and control the fraction of energy absorbed and scattered back, respectively, per unit distance in the layer of pigment. In the preferred embodiment of the present invention, three coefficients each are used for K and S, representing Red, Green, Blue (RGB) components of each quantity.\n\nSpecifying the Optical Properties of Pigments\n\nTypically, the K and S coefficients for a given colorant layer are determined experimentally, using spectral measurements from layers of known thicknesses. However, in the preferred embodiment of the present invention, it was much more convenient to allow a user to specify the K and S coefficients interactively, by choosing the desired appearance of a \u201cunit thickness\u201d of the pigment over both a white and a black background. Given these two user-selected RGB colors Rw and Rb, respectively, the K and S values can be computed by a simple inversion of the KM equations, as follows:  <!-- MathML: <math overflow=\"scroll\"> <mtable> <mtr> <mtd> <mrow> <mi>S</mi> <mo>=</mo> <mrow> <mfrac> <mn>1</mn> <mi>b</mi> </mfrac> <mo>\u00b7</mo> <mrow> <msup> <mi>coth</mi> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </msup> <mo>\ue8a0</mo> <mrow> <mo>(</mo> <mfrac> <mrow> <msup> <mi>b</mi> <mn>2</mn> </msup> <mo>-</mo> <mrow> <mrow> <mo>(</mo> <mrow> <mi>a</mi> <mo>-</mo> <msub> <mi>R</mi> <mi>w</mi> </msub> </mrow> <mo>)</mo> </mrow> <mo>\ue89e</mo> <mrow> <mo>(</mo> <mrow> <mi>a</mi> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> <mo>)</mo> </mrow> </mrow> </mrow> <mrow> <mi>b</mi> <mo>\ue8a0</mo> <mrow> <mo>(</mo> <mrow> <mn>1</mn> <mo>-</mo> <msub> <mi>R</mi> <mi>w</mi> </msub> </mrow> <mo>)</mo> </mrow> </mrow> </mfrac> <mo>)</mo> </mrow> </mrow> </mrow> </mrow> </mtd> <mtd> <mrow> <mo>(</mo> <mn>3</mn> <mo>)</mo> </mrow> </mtd> </mtr> </mtable> </math> --> S =   1 b  \u00b7   coth  - 1   \ue8a0  (    b 2  -   (  a -  R w   )  \ue89e  (  a - 1  )     b \ue8a0  (  1 -  R w   )    )        ( 3 )\n\n[![Figure US06198489-20010306-M00002](https://patentimages.storage.googleapis.com/fc/09/14/080513d595a89c/US06198489-20010306-M00002.png)](https://patentimages.storage.googleapis.com/fc/09/14/080513d595a89c/US06198489-20010306-M00002.png)\n\n    <!-- MathML: <math overflow=\"scroll\"> <mtable> <mtr> <mtd> <mrow> <mi>a</mi> <mo>=</mo> <mrow> <mfrac> <mn>1</mn> <mn>2</mn> </mfrac> <mo>\ue89e</mo> <mrow> <mo>(</mo> <mrow> <msub> <mi>R</mi> <mi>w</mi> </msub> <mo>+</mo> <mfrac> <mrow> <msub> <mi>R</mi> <mi>b</mi> </msub> <mo>-</mo> <msub> <mi>R</mi> <mi>w</mi> </msub> <mo>+</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> <msub> <mi>R</mi> <mi>b</mi> </msub> </mfrac> </mrow> <mo>)</mo> </mrow> </mrow> </mrow> </mtd> <mtd> <mrow> <mo>(</mo> <mn>5</mn> <mo>)</mo> </mrow> </mtd> </mtr> <mtr> <mtd> <mrow> <mi>b</mi> <mo>=</mo> <msqrt> <mrow> <msup> <mi>a</mi> <mn>2</mn> </msup> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </msqrt> </mrow> </mtd> <mtd> <mrow> <mo>(</mo> <mn>6</mn> <mo>)</mo> </mrow> </mtd> </mtr> </mtable> </math> --> a =   1 2  \ue89e  (   R w  +    R b  -  R w  + 1   R b    )       ( 5 )       b =    a 2  - 1       ( 6 )\n\n[![Figure US06198489-20010306-M00003](https://patentimages.storage.googleapis.com/2c/2f/be/c8e04f6244eb11/US06198489-20010306-M00003.png)](https://patentimages.storage.googleapis.com/2c/2f/be/c8e04f6244eb11/US06198489-20010306-M00003.png)\n\nThe above computations are applied to each color channel of S, K, R, and Rb independently. In order to avoid any divisions by zero, it is required that Rw>Rb for each color channel. (This restriction is reasonable even for opaque pigments, since the user is specifying reflected colors through just a thin layer, which should still be at least partially transparent.) While for most valid combinations of specified colors, the computed K and S values fall in the legal range of 0 to 1, for certain very saturated input colors, the scattering coefficient may actually exceed the value of 1 in some color channels. Though such a large value of S is clearly not possible for any physical pigment, no ill effects have been noted in the simulation from allowing such \u201cout-of-range\u201d values. (The situation is somewhat analogous to allowing an \u201calpha\u201d opacity to lie outside the range 0 to 1, another non-physical effect that is sometimes useful.)\n\nThis simple method of specifying pigments has been found to be quite adequate for creating a wide range of realistic paints. In addition, the method is much easier than taking the kind of extremely careful measurements that would otherwise be required. The method also appears to be much more intuitive for most people than specifying the K and S values directly. Note the following examples:\n\nOpaque paints, such as \u201cIndian red,\u201d exhibit a similar color on both white and black. Such paints exhibit high scattering in the same wavelengths as their color, and high absolption in complementary wavelengths.\n\nTransparent paints, such as \u201cQuinacridone rose,\u201d appear colored on white, and nearly black on black. Such paints have low scattering in all wavelengths, and high absorption in wavelengths complementary to their color.\n\nInterference paints, such as \u201cInterference lilac,\u201d appear white (or transparent) on white, and colored on black. Such paints have high scattering in the same wavelengths as their color, and low absorption in all wavelengths.\n\nIn the same way, this method also makes it easy to specify paints that exhibit slightly different hues over black than white, such as \u201cHansa yellow.\u201d Plate **5**H provides a visualization of the simulated color of this pigment as it is applied in layers of increasing thickness over both black and white backgrounds.\n\nOptical Compositing of Layers\n\nGiven scattering and absorption coefficients S and K for a pigmented layer of given thickness d at a particular pixel, the KM model enables the reflectance R and transmittance T through the layer to be determined as follows:\n\nR=sin *hb*Sd/*c* (7)\n\nT=*b/c* (8)\n\nwhere\n\n *c=a*sin *hb*SD+*b* cos *hb*SD (9)\n\nKubelka's optical compositing equations can then be used to determine the overall reflectance R and transmittance T of two abutting layers with reflectances R1, R2 and T1, T2, respectively: computation is repeated for each additional glaze. The overall reflectance R is then used to render the pixel.\n\nFor individual layers containing more than one pigment of thicknesses d1, . . . , dn, the S and K coefficients of each pigment i are weighted in proportion to that pigment's relative thickness di.         "is_missing": false,
        "text": "I developed and executed the watercolor look for David Gainey&#x27;s award-winning short, Fishing. A project that documents the way graffiti-covered walls change over time, using interactive timelapse collage. An optical sculpture that increases the distance between the viewer&#x27;s eyes. Originally built for the Burning Man art event in 2000. A short film that explains the miracle of reproduction using stop-motion animation and a lot of hot air. Photographed over a period of nine months in our home in San Francisco. An interactive web app designed to help neurotypical people understand what it&#x27;s like to have synaesthesia, and how my own synaesthesia interacts with attention and memory. Some excerpts from an abstract animation using synthesized light caustics, made using an AfterEffects plugin I created in 2002, and a real-time interactive Processing sketch I wrote in 2013.\n\nA project to stylize animation using not only the appearance of watercolor, but also its characteristic style of movement. I helped out on this one by providing some synthetic exemplars based on my watercolor simulation. A simulation of watercolor painting, including translucent glazing effects, pigment transport through shallow fluid across textured paper, and edge darkening due to evaporation. A technique for automatically rendering any 3D scene as loose ink lines with personality and style. A kinematic system for creating art-directed wrinkles on costumes for CG characters. I developed the first iteration of this system while working in the rigging department on Shrek. In 1997 and 1998, I taught the University of Washington's Capstone Animation class, an interdisciplinary course in computer animation production that brought together students from the Art, Music, and Computer Science departments to collaboratively create a short film.\n\nI developed and executed the watercolor look for David Gainey's award-winning short, Fishing. A project that documents the way graffiti-covered walls change over time, using interactive timelapse collage. An optical sculpture that increases the distance between the viewer's eyes. Originally built for the Burning Man art event in 2000. A short film that explains the miracle of reproduction using stop-motion animation and a lot of hot air. Photographed over a period of nine months in our home in San Francisco. An interactive web app designed to help neurotypical people understand what it's like to have synaesthesia, and how my own synaesthesia interacts with attention and memory.     "text": "Great news on all fronts. Let me lay this out clearly \u2014 no code changes yet since you wanted to review first.\n\n## Prior art landscape\n\n### Direct descendants of Curtis et al. 1997 (the same physically-based approach)\n\n- **Original paper page (Cassidy Curtis)** \u2014 https://grail.cs.washington.edu/projects/watercolor/ \u2014 the paper, PDFs, plates. Still maintained by U Washington's Graphics Lab.\n- **GPU Programming for Real-Time Watercolor Simulation** (Texas A&M thesis, ~2014) \u2014 https://oaktrust.library.tamu.edu/server/api/core/bitstreams/5575e4a6-40fd-4946-ad32-f83712ddc02f/content \u2014 full GPU implementation of Curtis et al. Closest academic counterpart to what we built.\n- **Real-Time Watercolor Simulation with Fluid Vorticity Within Brush Stroke** \u2014 https://www.researchgate.net/publication/321112340 \u2014 recent extension using vorticity confinement.\n- **University of Washington CRCS Interactive Watercolor Painting** (2023) \u2014 https://sites.uw.edu/crcs/projects/2023interactive-watercolor-painting/ \u2014 Lattice-Boltzmann variant + K-M, Unity implementation.\n\n### The other family: Tyler Hobbs's polygon-deformation approach (different algorithm, popular in JS land)\n\nThis is the one most JS \"watercolor\" libraries actually implement. It's not fluid simulation \u2014 it's recursive polygon distortion with alpha layering. Looks watercolor-ish, behaves nothing like ours.\n\n- **Tyler Hobbs original blog** \u2014 https://tylerxhobbs.com/essays/2017/a-generative-approach-to-simulating-watercolor-paints \u2014 the source.\n- **\"How to Hack a Painting\" (Strange Loop 2017)** \u2014 https://tylerxhobbs.com/essays/2020/how-to-hack-a-painting \u2014 the talk transcript.\n- **watercolorizer** \u2014 https://github.com/32bitkid/watercolorizer \u2014 JS implementation of Hobbs's approach.\n- **Hobbs JS gist** \u2014 https://gist.github.com/zapthedingbat/b50fb68ae290ac587b3d36bf14f1e6f3\n- **watercolors-generative (p5.js)** \u2014 https://github.com/MEECAH/watercolors-generative\n\n### JS canvas \"watercolor effects\" (not physically based; mostly visual styling)\n\n- **p5.brush** \u2014 https://github.com/topics/watercolor (top result, 589 stars) \u2014 custom brushes for p5.js, includes watercolor-ish flow fields. Not a fluid sim.\n- **Aquarelle (Ramotion)** \u2014 https://github.com/Ramotion/aquarelle \u2014 watercolor-effect *component* (image filter), not interactive paint.\n- **THREE.Watercolor** \u2014 https://github.com/mattatz/THREE.Watercolor \u2014 post-processing pass for three.js, paper-texture-driven, no fluid.\n- **jangmi-jo/watercolor** \u2014 https://github.com/jangmi-jo/watercolor \u2014 small canvas sketch app with gradient brushes.\n\n### What I didn't find\n\n**Nothing JS-based that implements Curtis et al.'s fluid+K-M model with real-time interaction in the browser.** The space we're in \u2014 interactive shallow-water-equation watercolor with multi-layer K-M compositing, running in JS at frame rate \u2014 appears to be genuinely empty. The closest comparable is Texas A&M's GPU thesis (C++ + GLSL, not interactive web). This is a meaningful gap to fill.\n\n## Legal \u2014 the actual answer, not lawyer-flavored hedging\n\n### Patent: expired Feb 18, 2018\n\n**US Patent 6,198,489 \u2014 \"Computer generated watercolor\"** \u2014 https://patents.google.com/patent/US6198489 \u2014 covers the three-layer water/pigment/paper model with K-M compositing.\n\n- Filed Feb 18, 1998 (with priority back to Feb 21, 1997 provisional)\n- Granted March 6, 2001\n- **Status on Google Patents: \"Expired - Lifetime\"**\n- **20-year term ran out Feb 18, 2018**\n\nThe patent has been in the public domain for over 8 years. The University of Washington (the assignee) cannot enforce an expired patent. **You're clear on the patent front.**\n\n### Copyright\n\nCopyright protects *expression* (specific code, specific text, specific images) \u2014 not *ideas*, *algorithms*, *mathematical methods*, or *facts*. This is 17 U.S.C. \u00a7 102(b) in the US, and it's the same in essentially every jurisdiction. You wrote (well, we wrote) all your own code from scratch, based on reading the paper and reasoning about the physics. That's exactly the canonical case copyright doesn't reach.\n\nThe K and S coefficients in the lib are taken from Curtis et al.'s Figure 5. Numerical measurements of physical pigments are facts, not creative expression \u2014 also not copyrightable. (Feist v. Rural Telephone Service, 1991, is the controlling case for this in the US.)\n\n### Citation / attribution\n\nWhat you absolutely should do (and the v0.73 docs already do correctly):\n- Cite the paper with full bibliographic info\n- Credit the authors by name\n- Make clear what your work draws on\n\nThis isn't a legal requirement \u2014 it's academic and engineering ethics, and it's how you signal \"I read the paper, I respect the work, I'm building on it.\" It also moves you firmly into \"scholarly reimplementation\" territory, which is the strongest defensive posture if anyone ever questioned anything.\n\n### Trademark\n\nNot a concern. \"Washes\" is a generic watercolor term and shouldn't conflict with anything. \"Curtis et al.\" is people's names cited academically.\n\n## Cease and desist risk: roughly zero\n\nThree reasons:\n\n1. **The authors are people, not a hostile corp.** Cassidy Curtis is at Google DeepMind as a Senior Staff Visual Designer \u2014 he keeps the paper available on his personal site (https://otherthings.com/portfolio/) and his Google Research page (https://research.google/people/cassidycurtis/), and writes about it positively. David Salesin was at Adobe Research, now at Google. Kurt Fleischer was at Pixar. These are senior, generous people who *want* their 28-year-old research cited. The U of Washington Graphics Lab still hosts the paper at https://grail.cs.washington.edu/projects/watercolor/ \u2014 they're proud of it.\n\n2. **No active enforcement entity.** Patent expired, assignee was a university (UW), university tech transfer offices don't pursue expired-patent claims because there's nothing to claim.\n\n3. **You're doing it the legitimate way.** Original implementation, full citation, OSS, no commercial deception. The legal categories that get C&Ds are: trademark infringement, copyright on copied code, active patents, defamation. You hit none of these.\n\nThe realistic worst case isn't a C&D \u2014 it's an email from Cassidy saying \"cool project, btw I noticed X.\" Which would be a good email to get.\n\n## OSS recommendation\n\nYes, definitely. **MIT or Apache-2.0** are both fine. A few thoughts:\n\n- **MIT** is simpler, no patent grant clause. Fine since the underlying patent is expired anyway.\n- **Apache-2.0** has an explicit patent grant from contributors, which is the more defensive choice if anyone ever adds code. Slightly more legalese but the standard for projects that want to project \"we've thought about this.\"\n\nFor a personal project building on academic work, MIT is the customary choice and what most of the prior-art repos I found use.\n\nA few specific suggestions for the repo:\n\n- Top-level `README.md` with the 1-paragraph \"what is this\" + the citation block + license badge\n- `CITATION.cff` file (GitHub auto-renders these; well-established for academic-adjacent projects)\n- `ACKNOWLEDGMENTS.md` or a section in the README crediting Curtis, Salesin, Fleischer, Anderson, Seims, Kubelka, Munk, Stam, Foster, Metaxas, Bridson by name\n- Link to the original paper from the README\n- Keep the v0.73 References section in the docs \u2014 that's the right move\n\n## Hacker News positioning\n\nA few thoughts on framing for HN specifically:\n\n**What works on HN:**\n- \"I built X from a 1997 SIGGRAPH paper in pure JS, here's what I learned about the math\" \u2014 the meta-narrative of reimplementing a classic from first principles is HN catnip\n- The Dig Deeper section is already 80% of an HN-quality writeup\n- The \u221a2 anisotropy bug story is the kind of detail HN loves\n- \"Interactive demo in the title link\" performs well \u2014 make sure the demo runs immediately, no signup, no nothing\n\n**What doesn't work on HN:**\n- Anything that smells like \"look at my new framework\" \u2014 frame it as research-implementation, not product\n- Hiding the prior art \u2014 HN commenters will instantly link Tyler Hobbs and ask how you compare. Pre-empt with a \"How does this compare to\u2026\" section.\n\n**Title suggestions to consider:**\n- \"Washes: a JS watercolor simulation, built from Curtis et al.'s 1997 SIGGRAPH paper\"\n- \"I spent thirteen versions chasing a \u221a2 in my watercolor simulation\"\n- \"Watercolor in the browser, the slow way: implementing a 1997 fluid-sim paper\"\n\nThe middle one is the HN-shaped one. People love a good debugging postmortem.\n\n## What I'd recommend doing next\n\n1. **Verify on your own** \u2014 at least skim https://patents.google.com/patent/US6198489 (look at \"Legal status: Expired - Lifetime\") and Wikipedia's article on patent terms if you want to feel confident\n2. **Optionally** email Cassidy Curtis at his Google Research page before posting. Subject like \"JS reimplementation of your 1997 watercolor paper \u2014 wanted to share.\" 95% he'll be flattered. 5% he asks for a small thing. 0% he says don't. This isn't legally required but it's professionally graceful and many HN commenters notice the difference.\n3. **Pick a license**, add the standard OSS scaffolding files\n4. **Once decided**, I can add a \"Prior art\" section to the docs and the licensing/citation files to the repo\n\nIf you want me to draft any of these \u2014 the README, CITATION.cff, prior art section in the docs, or the email to Cassidy \u2014 say the word.",
